**Solihull** ([/ˈsɒlihʌl, ˈsoʊl-, ˌsoʊliˈhʌl/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*SO(H)L-ee-hul, SOH-lee-HUL*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key")) is a large [market town](/wiki/Market_town "Market town") in the east of the [West Midlands county](/wiki/West_Midlands_(county) "West Midlands (county)"), England. Solihull is situated on the [River Blythe](/wiki/River_Blythe "River Blythe") in the [Forest of Arden](/wiki/Arden,_Warwickshire "Arden, Warwickshire") area. It is the most important town in the [Metropolitan Borough of Solihull](/wiki/Metropolitan_Borough_of_Solihull "Metropolitan Borough of Solihull"), which is named after it. The town had a population of 126,577 at the 2021 Census, and the wider metropolitan borough had a population of 216,240.[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) The town is located around 8 miles (13 km) southeast of [Birmingham](/wiki/Birmingham "Birmingham") and 14 miles (23 km) west of [Coventry](/wiki/Coventry "Coventry"). Solihull is one of the major towns of the [West Midlands conurbation](/wiki/West_Midlands_conurbation "West Midlands conurbation") centred around Birmingham.

Solihull itself is mostly urban; however, the larger borough is rural in character, with many outlying villages, and three quarters of the borough designated as [green belt](/wiki/Green_belt "Green belt").[[3]](#cite_note-3) The town and its borough was part of [Warwickshire](/wiki/Warwickshire "Warwickshire") for most of its history.[[4]](#cite_note-historic-county-4) It has roots dating back to the 1st century BC, and was further formally established during the medieval era.[*[vague](/wiki/Wikipedia:Vagueness "Wikipedia:Vagueness")*] Today the town is famed as, amongst other things, the birthplace of the [Land Rover](/wiki/Land_Rover "Land Rover") car marque, home of Solihull Moors FC and the training facilities for the [British Equestrian](/wiki/British_Equestrian "British Equestrian") teams. It became part of the [West Midlands metropolitan county](/wiki/West_Midlands_(county) "West Midlands (county)") in 1974.

## Toponym

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Solihull&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Toponym")]

Solihull's name is commonly thought to have derived from the position of its [arden stone](/wiki/Arden,_Warwickshire#Arden_Stone "Arden, Warwickshire") parish church, St Alphege, on a 'soily' hill.[[5]](#cite_note-5) The church was built on a hill of stiff red [marl](/wiki/Marl "Marl"), which turned to sticky mud in wet weather.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Solihull&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

### Early history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Solihull&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Early history")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/63/Lines_family_sketchbook_-_River_cole.jpg/250px-Lines_family_sketchbook_-_River_cole.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lines_family_sketchbook_-_River_cole.jpg)

Wooden bridge over the Cole at Shirley, drawn in the 19th century by [Samuel Rostill Lines](/wiki/Samuel_Rostill_Lines "Samuel Rostill Lines").

The land now forming Solihull was once covered in the ancient [Forest of Arden](/wiki/Arden,_Warwickshire "Arden, Warwickshire").

The earliest known settlement in the area was at [Berry Mound](/wiki/Berry_Mound "Berry Mound"), [Shirley](/wiki/Shirley,_West_Midlands "Shirley, West Midlands"), which was the site of an Iron Age Hill Fort, a fortified village protected by earth banks, dating back to the 1st century BC and which covered approximately 11 acres (4.5 ha).[[6]](#cite_note-6) The name Shirley means either 'a bright clearing' or 'a border clearing' in the Forest of Arden.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

During the later [Iron Age](/wiki/Iron_Age "Iron Age") the [River Cole](/wiki/River_Cole,_West_Midlands "River Cole, West Midlands"), which feeds the River Blythe, is believed to have been the border between the [Corieltauvi](/wiki/Corieltauvi "Corieltauvi") and the [Cornovii](/wiki/Cornovii_(Midlands) "Cornovii (Midlands)"), with Solihull forming the junction of the two powerful Celtic Tribes.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

Throughout the [Roman occupation](/wiki/Roman_Britain "Roman Britain") of Britain it was held that no Roman roads made it through the Forest of Arden because it was so dense. The nearest known major Roman settlements being at [Grimstock Hill](/wiki/Grimstock_Hill_Romano-British_settlement "Grimstock Hill Romano-British settlement") on the Solihull border, [Metchley Fort](/wiki/Metchley_Fort "Metchley Fort") (around 8.5 miles north west), and [Alcester](/wiki/Alcester "Alcester") (around 15 miles south).

### Anglo Saxon era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Solihull&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Anglo Saxon era")]

By the Anglo Saxon era, the forest of Arden was part of the [Kingdom of Mercia](/wiki/Mercia "Mercia"). An [assart](/wiki/Assarting "Assarting") settlement known as the manor of Ulverlei, meaning 'Wulfhere's clearing' was established, with its centre north east of the hillfort at Shirely. [Wulfhere](/wiki/Wulfhere_of_Mercia "Wulfhere of Mercia") was the [first Christian King of all Mercia](/wiki/List_of_monarchs_of_Mercia "List of monarchs of Mercia"). The settlement was a clearing in the dense woodland of the Forest of Arden, with the land farmed in common.[[9]](#cite_note-auto8-9) The older settlement at Shirely was considered part of the new Manor of Ulverlei. This status as a clearing in the countryside is still reflected to this day in the town motto, "Urbs in rure" or "town in the country".[[10]](#cite_note-10)

Local [folklore](/wiki/Folklore "Folklore") holds that as part of his [campaigns against the Viking invasion](/wiki/Viking_activity_in_the_British_Isles#England "Viking activity in the British Isles") in the [mid 9th century](/wiki/9th_century "9th century") [Alfred the Great](/wiki/Alfred_the_Great "Alfred the Great") fought a battle against the [Danes](/wiki/Danelaw "Danelaw") at Berry Mound, Shirley.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

After the absorption of Mercia into the rest of England, Ulverlei became the property of the Earls of Mercia. The first of these was [Leofric](/wiki/Leofric,_Earl_of_Mercia "Leofric, Earl of Mercia"), husband of [Lady Godiva](/wiki/Lady_Godiva "Lady Godiva"), heroine of the Warwickshire legend. The manor of Ulverlei later passed to Leofric's grandson, [Edwin, Earl of Mercia](/wiki/Edwin,_Earl_of_Mercia "Edwin, Earl of Mercia") who held it until his death in 1071. Leofric's great-nephew, Thorkell of Arden, would become progenitor of the locally prominent [Arden family](/wiki/Arden_family "Arden family"), one of the few Anglo Saxon families to retain their land holdings after the [Norman Conquest](/wiki/Norman_Conquest "Norman Conquest"), and eventually settling in their primary estate in [Castle Bromwich](/wiki/Castle_Bromwich "Castle Bromwich"), today in the Borough of Solihull.

### Early medieval era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Solihull&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Early medieval era")]

In 1086, it was recorded that the Manor of Ulverlei was now held by [Cristina](/wiki/Cristina_(daughter_of_Edward_the_Exile) "Cristina (daughter of Edward the Exile)"), great-granddaughter of [Ethelred the Unready](/wiki/%C3%86thelred_the_Unready "Æthelred the Unready"), daughter of [Edward the Exile](/wiki/Edward_the_Exile "Edward the Exile"), and sister of the last Anglo Saxon King [Edgar Aetheling](/wiki/Edgar_%C3%86theling "Edgar Ætheling").[[12]](#cite_note-12) Shortly after 1086, Christina entered the nunnery of Romsey Abbey in Hampshire. Her lands were granted to the Norman [Ralph de Limesy](/wiki/Ralph_de_Limesy "Ralph de Limesy"). The extent of the area historically considered the manor of Ulverlei is demarked by an area called 'Worlds End', a historical naming practice indicating that people did not live beyond there.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

It was between 1170 and 1180 that the de Limsey family founded the settlement of 'Solihull' as a "planted borough" or planned village to the south of Ulverli. It was called a borough simply because the de Limsey Lord of the Manor offered free [burgage](/wiki/Burgage "Burgage") tenure where residents were free, rent-paying burgesses, rather than [villeins](/wiki/Villein "Villein") owing service to the Lord of the Manor. By the time of [Edward I](/wiki/Edward_I_of_England "Edward I of England"), Ulverlie was [sub-infeudated](/wiki/Subinfeudation "Subinfeudation") into the newly created Manor of Solihull, and became known as the 'Old Town', contracted to its present name, [Olton](/wiki/Olton "Olton") to distinguish itself from the New Town of Solihull.[[9]](#cite_note-auto8-9)

The de Limsey family held the Manor of Solihull, until Ralph's great-granddaughter married Hugh de Odingsells, whose family were thought to be of Flemish origin.

The Odingsells were the Lords of the Manor of Ulverley, and later after its subinfeudation, Solihull, from the 12th century and are believed to have constructed a castle on the site now known as [Hobs Moat](/wiki/Hobs_Moat "Hobs Moat") (a possible corruption of Odingsells' Moat). The castle was occupied until around the 14th century.[[14]](#cite_note-auto3-14) The Odingsells were relatives of the powerful Clinton [Earls of Huntingdon](/wiki/William_de_Clinton,_1st_Earl_of_Huntingdon "William de Clinton, 1st Earl of Huntingdon") of [Maxstoke Castle](/wiki/Maxstoke_Castle "Maxstoke Castle") (around 8 miles north east of Hobs Moat), whose relatives would also control nearby [Coleshill Manor](/wiki/Coleshill,_Warwickshire "Coleshill, Warwickshire") (around 6 miles north east of Hobs Moat), [Kenilworth Castle](/wiki/Kenilworth_Castle "Kenilworth Castle") (around 13 miles south east of Hobs Moat) and [Baddesley Clinton](/wiki/Baddesley_Clinton "Baddesley Clinton") (around 8 miles south of Hobs Moat).

The red [sandstone](/wiki/Sandstone "Sandstone") [parish church](/wiki/Parish_church "Parish church") of [St. Alphege](/wiki/Alphege "Alphege") dates from a similar period to Hobs Moat and is a large and handsome example of English Gothic church [architecture](/wiki/Architecture "Architecture"), with a traditional [spire](/wiki/Spire "Spire") 168 feet (51 metres) high, making it visible from a great distance. It is located at the head of High Street and is a Grade I [listed building](/wiki/Listed_building "Listed building").[[15]](#cite_note-15) It was founded in about 1220 by Hugh de Oddingsell. A [chantry](/wiki/Chantry "Chantry") chapel was also founded there by Sir William de Oddingsell in 1277 and the upper chapel in St Alphege was built for a chantry.

By 1242, the Manor of Solihull was granted a Royal charter to hold a weekly market and an annual fair "on the vigil, the feast and the morrow of St Alphege" (18-20 April).[[9]](#cite_note-auto8-9) It was around this time that Solihull became a hub for its surrounding parishes.

The town of Solihull would later absorb the nearby settlement of [Longdon](/wiki/Longdon,_Solihull "Longdon, Solihull"). The first recorded reference to Longdon was in 1086 as 'Langedone', meaning the 'long hill'. The 'long hill' in question was the hill on what is now Solihull's Marsh Lane and Yew Tree Lane, leading from the [River Blythe](/wiki/River_Blythe "River Blythe") up onto [Elmdon Heath](/wiki/Elmdon_Heath "Elmdon Heath"). The Longdon Manor House was at its edge on Copt Heath.[[16]](#cite_note-16) In 1161 the [Manor](/wiki/Manorialism "Manorialism") of Longdon had been property of [Ketelberne de Langdon](/w/index.php?title=Ketelberne_de_Langdon&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ketelberne de Langdon (page does not exist)"), who founded [Henwood Priory](/wiki/Henwood_Priory "Henwood Priory") and gave his name to the settlement of [Catherine-de-Barnes](/wiki/Catherine-de-Barnes "Catherine-de-Barnes") (a corruption of the name Ketelberne). The assimilation of Longdon into Solihull was so total that few references exist today indicating it was ever a separate place.

The Longdon area bordered onto the settlement of [Hampton in Arden](/wiki/Hampton_in_Arden "Hampton in Arden"), appearing in the Domesday Survey of 1086 as 'Hantone'.[[17]](#cite_note-auto4-17) Despite bordering Solihull, Hampton in Arden would not be incorporated into the borough of Solihull until later. From the middle of the 12th century Hampton in Arden was owned by the [de Arden](/wiki/Arden_family "Arden family") family, and also included the then hamlet of [Knowle](/wiki/Knowle,_West_Midlands "Knowle, West Midlands"). Knowle would become a [royal manor](/wiki/Royal_manor "Royal manor") in 1285 when the de Arden family sold it to King Edward I and Queen Eleanor. In 1396, Walter Cook applied for a faculty to build a church in 'Knoll', so the villagers would no longer have to cross the treacherous waters of the river Blythe to get to church, and this was granted by Pope Boniface IX on 4 May 1396. By 1402 the church was consecrated and Knowle broke away from Hampton in Arden, later becoming part of the borough of Solihull.[[18]](#cite_note-18)[[17]](#cite_note-auto4-17)

Near Knowle lies the settlement of [Temple Balsall](/wiki/Temple_Balsall "Temple Balsall"), part of the borough of Solihull, that was founded by the [Knights Templar](/wiki/Knights_Templar "Knights Templar"), who farmed about 650 acres (2.6 km2) of the estate in the 12th century, and established the church and the Balsall Preceptory.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

### Later medieval era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Solihull&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Later medieval era")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/95/Blason_Thomas_Le_Despencer.svg/250px-Blason_Thomas_Le_Despencer.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Blason_Thomas_Le_Despencer.svg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8b/Wm_Trussell_Boroughbridge_Rebel_Arms.jpg/250px-Wm_Trussell_Boroughbridge_Rebel_Arms.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wm_Trussell_Boroughbridge_Rebel_Arms.jpg)

Rivals: The arms of Hugh Despenser, [Chamberlain](/wiki/Chamberlain_(office) "Chamberlain (office)") to the King and Lord of the Manor of Solihull (left) and William Trussell, King's [Secretary of State](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_(England) "Secretary of State (England)") and Lord of the Manor of Nuthurst (right).

By 1295, the Manor of Solihull had passed from the Odingsells via heiress to the [de Birmingham family](/wiki/De_Birmingham_family "De Birmingham family"), however they in turn quickly passed it into the [Le Despencer](/wiki/Despencer "Despencer") family. The Despencers found themselves at odds with the [Trussell family](/wiki/William_Trussell "William Trussell") of nearby [Nuthurst](/wiki/Hockley_Heath "Hockley Heath") (now in the borough of Solihull) during the baronial revolt under [Edward II](/wiki/Edward_II "Edward II"). The warring between the two families ended when Sir William Trussell of Nuthurst led the invasion of England by [Queen Isabella](/wiki/Isabella_of_France "Isabella of France") against Edward II, which installed [Edward III](/wiki/Edward_III_of_England "Edward III of England") on the throne. [Hugh Despenser](/wiki/Hugh_Despenser_the_Younger "Hugh Despenser the Younger") was executed and Sir William Trussell was made the [King's Secretary of State for England](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_(England) "Secretary of State (England)") under Edward III. The rivalry between the two families was intense, and at one point the grandson of Sir William Trussell, Sir John Trussell, abducted the widow of Hugh Despenser's grandson (also called [Hugh Despenser](/wiki/Hugh_Despenser_(died_1374) "Hugh Despenser (died 1374)")), after he was killed in battle, and forced her to marry him in an ultimately futile attempt to take the manor of Solihull. The Despencers would briefly fall back into favour some years later when helping with the campaigns of [Edward the Black Prince](/wiki/Edward_the_Black_Prince "Edward the Black Prince").[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-auto5-21)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/64/Hobs_Moat_Ancient_Monument_-_geograph.org.uk_-_85091.jpg/250px-Hobs_Moat_Ancient_Monument_-_geograph.org.uk_-_85091.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hobs_Moat_Ancient_Monument_-_geograph.org.uk_-_85091.jpg)

Hobs Moat Castle, [Olton](/wiki/Olton "Olton"), Solihull

It is during this time in the 14th century that Hobs Moat Castle is believed to have fallen into ruin. Antiquary [Sir William Dugdale](/wiki/William_Dugdale "William Dugdale") would visit the site three centuries later in 1656, finding only "a large Moat" and was informed by the locals that the castle there had long since been removed.[[14]](#cite_note-auto3-14) The Odingsells had built a new purpose-built [manor house](/wiki/Manor_house "Manor house") closer to the new town centre, called [Silhill Hall](/w/index.php?title=Silhill_Hall&action=edit&redlink=1 "Silhill Hall (page does not exist)"), at some point in the 13th century.[[22]](#cite_note-22) It is believed that through much of their ownership of the manor the Despencers would rent out Silhill Hall, or have their [stewards](/wiki/Steward_(office) "Steward (office)") reside there.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

Within the Longdon area of Solihull, is an area known as Malvern, named for Simon de Malverne, believed to be of [Malvern in Worcestershire](/wiki/Malvern,_Worcestershire "Malvern, Worcestershire"), assassinated in 1317. A moated site opposite the end of Marsh Lane is believed to be the de Malvern former home.[[24]](#cite_note-auto7-24)

By the 14th century, the town had become famed for its blacksmiths, and the Solihull High Street was known as le Smythestret.[[25]](#cite_note-auto1-25) This was because of its location in the Forest of Arden, and the abundance of trees required to provide fuel for the fires.[[26]](#cite_note-26) The town was also known for textiles. The end of Drury Lane was known as Teinters Green, believed to be an area where cloth was stretched on tenter hooks.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

In the grounds of St Alphege church is a now [Grade II](/wiki/Grade_II "Grade II") listed ruin dating from the 14th century. It is believed to have been a well house for a [holy spring](/wiki/Holy_well "Holy well"), and later possibly an [Oratory](/wiki/Oratory_(worship) "Oratory (worship)").[[28]](#cite_note-28)

Historians have suggested that the Shakespeare family, ancestors of [William Shakespeare](/wiki/William_Shakespeare "William Shakespeare") (born a few miles south in [Stratford-upon-Avon](/wiki/Stratford-upon-Avon "Stratford-upon-Avon")), were originally from Solihull's [Balsall](/wiki/Balsall_Common "Balsall Common"), with their names appearing in local registers between 1385 and 1457.[[29]](#cite_note-29) Shakespeare's mother, [Mary Arden](/wiki/Mary_Shakespeare "Mary Shakespeare"), was from a [cadet branch](/wiki/Cadet_branch "Cadet branch") of the [de Arden family](/wiki/Arden_family "Arden family") of [Castle Bromwich](/wiki/Castle_Bromwich "Castle Bromwich") in the borough of Solihull.[[30]](#cite_note-30) Shakespeare's son [Hamnet Shakespeare](/wiki/Hamnet_Shakespeare "Hamnet Shakespeare")'s baptism on 23 March 1560 is recorded in the Register of Solihull.

In 1400, [Thomas Despenser, 1st Earl of Gloucester](/wiki/Thomas_Despenser,_1st_Earl_of_Gloucester "Thomas Despenser, 1st Earl of Gloucester") died a traitor for his part in the [Epiphany Rising](/wiki/Epiphany_Rising "Epiphany Rising"), and so the Crown took custody of the manor because the heir, Richard le Despenser, was a minor.[[21]](#cite_note-auto5-21)

### Early modern era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Solihull&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Early modern era")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3c/Solihull_Manor_house.jpg/250px-Solihull_Manor_house.jpg)](/wiki/File:Solihull_Manor_house.jpg)

Lime Tree House, also erroneously known as "The Manor House", Solihull

The manor of Solihull remained property of the king for many years, and was passed through a number of custodians or lessees. In 1495 the Greswolde family of Solihull and nearby Kenilworth, while serving as the Kings custodians of the manor of Solihull, built the house on the Solihull High Street called Lime Tree House now erroneously known as the 'Manor House' (as no [lord of the manor](/wiki/Lord_of_the_manor "Lord of the manor") ever lived there - Silhill Hall was the manor house for Solihull).

The manor of Solihull was eventually granted to the [Duke of Norfolk](/wiki/Thomas_Howard,_3rd_Duke_of_Norfolk "Thomas Howard, 3rd Duke of Norfolk"), who in turn passed it on in 1530 to the local [Throckmorton family](/wiki/Throckmorton_baronets "Throckmorton baronets") of [Coughton Court](/wiki/Coughton_Court "Coughton Court"). Solihull was owned by the Throckmortons during the [Throckmorton Plot](/wiki/Throckmorton_Plot "Throckmorton Plot") of 1583, and the planning of the 1605 [Gunpowder Plot](/wiki/Gunpowder_Plot "Gunpowder Plot") to blow up Parliament in which the Throckmorton's were heavily involved. Their co-conspirators included the [Catesby family](/wiki/Robert_Catesby "Robert Catesby") of [Lapworth](/wiki/Lapworth "Lapworth") (then part of the borough of Solihull), and the [Digby](/wiki/Everard_Digby "Everard Digby") family who were Lords of the Manor of [Coleshill](/wiki/Coleshill,_Warwickshire "Coleshill, Warwickshire") (much of which is part of the borough of Solihull today). They rented a house in London from Henry Ferrers of [Baddesley Clinton](/wiki/Baddesley_Clinton "Baddesley Clinton") (then in the borough of Solihull) to store the gunpowder. [Sir Richard Walsh](/wiki/Richard_Walsh_(English_politician) "Richard Walsh (English politician)"), owner of [Walsh Hall](/wiki/Walsh_Hall,_Meriden "Walsh Hall, Meriden") in [Meriden](/wiki/Meriden,_West_Midlands "Meriden, West Midlands") (part of the modern borough of Solihull) was the Sheriff of Worcestershire who eventually cornered and killed the gunpowder plotters.[[21]](#cite_note-auto5-21) John Greswolde, brother of [Robert Greswolde](/wiki/Robert_Grissold "Robert Grissold"), of the wider Greswolde family of Solihull, was an attendant of [Henry Garnet](/wiki/Henry_Garnet "Henry Garnet") (a priest executed for his complicity in the Gunpowder Plot of 1605). It was said that when John was arrested and interrogated after the Gunpowder plot he was [racked](/wiki/Rack_(torture) "Rack (torture)") so badly at the [Tower of London](/wiki/Tower_of_London "Tower of London") that it was rumoured he was dead.[[31]](#cite_note-31)[[32]](#cite_note-32)

The sub-manor of Longdon in Solihull meanwhile had passed to the Greswolde family, in the era of [Elizabeth I](/wiki/Elizabeth_I "Elizabeth I"), however it soon passed through a daughter to [Thomas Dabridgecourt](/wiki/Thomas_Dabridgecourt "Thomas Dabridgecourt"). In 1680 the Rev. Henry Greswolde, then rector of Solihull, bought a large farm called Malvern Farm, in the Malvern part of Longdon Manor, to establish a new family seat, which would be called Malvern Hall.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/Solihull_Town_Centre_-_geograph.org.uk_-_69337.jpg/250px-Solihull_Town_Centre_-_geograph.org.uk_-_69337.jpg)](/wiki/File:Solihull_Town_Centre_-_geograph.org.uk_-_69337.jpg)

Historic Solihull town centre

In 1604 the Throckmorton family sold the Manor of Solihull to Edmund Hawes. The Hawes family were already prominent local landowners, having owned the Hillfields area of Solihull since 1311, when Thomas Hawes, a lawyer, purchased the land. William & Ursula Hawes had constructed [Hillfield Hall](/w/index.php?title=Hillfield_Hall&action=edit&redlink=1 "Hillfield Hall (page does not exist)") in 1576, a grand Tudor hall with battlements and a long gallery. The hall oversaw five farms, including Hillfields and Shelly farm. William Hawes lived there until his death in 1611.[[34]](#cite_note-auto6-34) Shelly had been a thriving hamlet during the 13th and 14th centuries, and included [Monkspath](/wiki/Monkspath "Monkspath"), but there was little mention of the area by the 17th century.[[35]](#cite_note-35) Hillfield Hall remains residential to this day, while the 16th-century Grade II listed Shelly farmhouse is an upmarket bar and restaurant.[[36]](#cite_note-36)

The historic [Solihull School](/wiki/Solihull_School "Solihull School") was also founded in 1560 (although not on its present site). On the right along High Street from [St Alphege Church](/wiki/St_Alphege_Church,_Solihull "St Alphege Church, Solihull") porch is one of the town's oldest landmarks, the George, a hotel which dates from the 16th century.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/Hillfield_Hall_Court-geograph-3703111-by-Tiger.jpg/250px-Hillfield_Hall_Court-geograph-3703111-by-Tiger.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hillfield_Hall_Court-geograph-3703111-by-Tiger.jpg)

Hillfield Hall

Solihull appears to have survived the [English Civil War](/wiki/English_Civil_War "English Civil War") of 1642–1651 relatively unscathed, even though many important close engagements and battles were fought nearby including the [Battle of Edgehill](/wiki/Battle_of_Edgehill "Battle of Edgehill") to the south and the [Battle of Camp Hill](/wiki/Battle_of_Camp_Hill "Battle of Camp Hill") to the north. The very first skirmish between the Roundheads and Cavaliers took place just north of Solihull in the fields to the south of the nearby settlement of [Curdworth](/wiki/Curdworth "Curdworth") in Warwickshire, called the Battle of Curdworth Bridge. Recent archaeological excavations unearthed evidence that a battle may have been fought at Coleshill Manor near the Solihull border. It is reported that [Prince Rupert](/wiki/Prince_Rupert_of_the_Rhine "Prince Rupert of the Rhine"), commander of the Royalist forces, was on his way to meet [King Charles](/wiki/Charles_I_of_England "Charles I of England") in Solihull when he was skirmished by Parliamentary forces in the [Battle of Kings Norton](/wiki/Battle_of_Kings_Norton "Battle of Kings Norton").[[37]](#cite_note-37)[[38]](#cite_note-38)

Before its eventual sale to the Greswoldes as part of the Manor of Longdon, the Malvern area of Solihull had been the property of Parliamentarian commander [Robert Greville](/wiki/Robert_Greville,_2nd_Baron_Brooke "Robert Greville, 2nd Baron Brooke").[[24]](#cite_note-auto7-24) The Greville family remained important to the area, with [Fulke Greville, 5th Baron Brooke](/wiki/Baron_Brooke "Baron Brooke") being erroneously recorded as the Lord of the Manor of Longdon in 1682.[[21]](#cite_note-auto5-21) A branch of the Greville family would make donations for schools in Knowle in the early 1700s.[[39]](#cite_note-39)

### Modern era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Solihull&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Modern era")]

In the modern era, Solihull became quieter, with textile working and iron working still prominent in the town.[[25]](#cite_note-auto1-25) The Manor of Solihull continued to pass through a number of other holders including the Archer family of [Umberslade Hall](/wiki/Umberslade_Hall "Umberslade Hall") in [Nuthurst](/wiki/Hockley_Heath "Hockley Heath"). Eventually in 1850 it passed to [Robert Short](/wiki/Robert_Short_(East_India_Company_officer) "Robert Short (East India Company officer)") an officer in the [East India Company](/wiki/East_India_Company "East India Company"), who left it to his son in law, Rev. John Couchman. The Couchman's remain the Lords of the Manor of Solihull to the present day.[[40]](#cite_note-40)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/John_Constable_-_Malvern_Hall%2C_Warwickshire_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg/250px-John_Constable_-_Malvern_Hall%2C_Warwickshire_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)](/wiki/File:John_Constable_-_Malvern_Hall,_Warwickshire_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)

*[Malvern Hall](/wiki/Malvern_Hall_(painting) "Malvern Hall (painting)")* by [John Constable](/wiki/John_Constable "John Constable"), 1821.

By the 1700s, work was underway on Malvern Hall, with Humphrey Greswolde overseeing, it would be completed in the first half of the century. The hall featured a gatehouse onto the Warwick Road designed by [Sir John Soane](/wiki/John_Soane "John Soane"), architect of the Bank of England.[[41]](#cite_note-41) In 1809 the hall would be painted by [John Constable](/wiki/John_Constable "John Constable"), for his patron, Henry Greswolde Lewis.[[42]](#cite_note-42) The Greswoldes would later sell the house to Solihull council who converted the estate into [Malvern Park](/wiki/Malvern_and_Brueton_Park "Malvern and Brueton Park"). The hall is today part of Solihull School.

Also constructed in 1712, was Touchwood Hall at the end of Drury Lane on Teinters Green. The hall would serve as the home of the [Holbeche family](/wiki/Holbeche_family "Holbeche family"), former lords of the manor at [Widney Manor](/wiki/Widney_Manor "Widney Manor") and a prominent local family. The hall would later be held by the Madeley and Martineau families. The hall was demolished in 1963 but lends its name to Solihull's famous shopping centre.[[43]](#cite_note-43)

During this time poets [William Shenstone](/wiki/William_Shenstone "William Shenstone") and [Richard Jago](/wiki/Richard_Jago "Richard Jago") attended Solihull School, where today, two houses are named after them.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/60/Solihull_Malvern_Hall.JPG/250px-Solihull_Malvern_Hall.JPG)](/wiki/File:Solihull_Malvern_Hall.JPG)

Solihull Malvern Hall

In 1775, [Monkspath Hall](/wiki/Monkspath_Hall "Monkspath Hall") was constructed. It captured the nation's media attention two centuries later when it was illegally demolished, sparking a court case which demanded it be put back exactly as it was.

In 1785, the [Earl of Aylesford](/wiki/Earl_of_Aylesford "Earl of Aylesford") founded, and became patron of, the [Woodmen of Arden](/wiki/Woodmen_of_Arden "Woodmen of Arden"). This is a prestigious society of Toxophilites who meet to shoot [longbows](/wiki/Longbow "Longbow") at their ground in the Forest of Arden in Meriden, in the borough of Solihull. The Woodmen target shoot at only one distance, 100 yards, and score in the Archers disfavour.[[44]](#cite_note-44) Archery had always been an important sport in Solihull. On the doorway of the church of St Alphage are incisions which are arrow sharpening marks from the 1360s when men were required to practice archery on a Sunday to ensure a ready supply of archers. The long marks have been made by Broadheads, the round by Bodkins - types of arrowheads used with the long bows of the time.[[45]](#cite_note-45) The society is strictly limited to a membership of 80, with this rule only having been bent once, when in 1835 Prime Minister Sir Robert Peel, Lord of the Manor of Hampton in Arden, was added.[[46]](#cite_note-46)

From 1838 to 1839, the local Catholic church, [St Augustine of England Church](/wiki/St_Augustine_of_England_Church,_Solihull "St Augustine of England Church, Solihull") was built in Solihull. It was designed by [Augustus Pugin](/wiki/Augustus_Pugin "Augustus Pugin") and paid for by the [Pippet family of Solihull](/wiki/Pippet_family_of_Solihull "Pippet family of Solihull"), with stained-glass windows made by the notable Birmingham manufacturer [Hardman & Co.](/wiki/Hardman_%26_Co. "Hardman & Co."). In the late 19th century, further work was done on the church to the designs of [Charles Francis Hansom](/wiki/Charles_Francis_Hansom "Charles Francis Hansom").[[47]](#cite_note-47)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3f/Wilberforce_john_rising.jpg/250px-Wilberforce_john_rising.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wilberforce_john_rising.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b9/Robert_Peel_by_RR_Scanlan.jpg/250px-Robert_Peel_by_RR_Scanlan.jpg)](/wiki/File:Robert_Peel_by_RR_Scanlan.jpg)

During the 19th Century the area was home to the [country residences](/wiki/English_country_house "English country house") of abolitionist [William Wilberforce](/wiki/William_Wilberforce "William Wilberforce") (left) and Prime Minister [Sir Robert Peel](/wiki/Robert_Peel "Robert Peel") (right).

In the early 1800s, Isaac William Lillingston sold the Manor of Hampton in Arden to former [Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_the_United_Kingdom "Prime Minister of the United Kingdom"), and [founder of the modern police force](/wiki/Metropolitan_Police_Act_1829 "Metropolitan Police Act 1829"), [Sir Robert Peel](/wiki/Robert_Peel "Robert Peel"). Peel and his son [Sir Frederick Peel](/wiki/Frederick_Peel "Frederick Peel") modernised and made improvements to Hampton in Arden, including the construction of a new manor house, (which is today the Michelin starred [Peel's Restaurant](/wiki/Peel%27s_Restaurant "Peel's Restaurant")).[[17]](#cite_note-auto4-17)

It was also during the early 1800s that [abolitionist](/wiki/Abolitionism_in_the_United_Kingdom "Abolitionism in the United Kingdom") [William Wilberforce](/wiki/William_Wilberforce "William Wilberforce") moved to the [Elmdon](/wiki/Elmdon,_West_Midlands "Elmdon, West Midlands") area of Solihull following his marriage to [Barbara Spooner](/wiki/Barbara_Wilberforce "Barbara Wilberforce"), of the Spooner banking family who were the owners of Elmdon hall. A road - Wilberforce Way - is named for him north of Solihull town centre.[[48]](#cite_note-48)

The manor of Longdon came to famous poet [Lord Byron](/wiki/Lord_Byron "Lord Byron") in 1815 by his marriage with Anne, daughter of Sir Ralph Milbanke Noel, and on her death in 1860 passed to her grandson the [Earl of Lovelace](/wiki/Earl_of_Lovelace "Earl of Lovelace"). Two roads in Solihull are named after the family, Lady Byron Lane and Lovelace Avenue.[[49]](#cite_note-49)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/43/Dog_Kennel_Lane_Post_Box.JPG/250px-Dog_Kennel_Lane_Post_Box.JPG)](/wiki/File:Dog_Kennel_Lane_Post_Box.JPG)

Dog Kennel Lane Post Box

Longdon Hall, with its remaining manorial rights, was later bought by a solicitor, Mr. J. B. Clarke of Birmingham in 1899 and soon afterwards sold to Mr. Alfred Lovekin, a silversmith.[[21]](#cite_note-auto5-21) Mr Lovekin also leased land near Solihull town centre from the Chattock family of [Castle Bromwich](/wiki/Castle_Bromwich "Castle Bromwich"), on which he built the Jacobean style Tudor Grange Hall. After Mr Lovekin's death, Tudor Grange Hall was sold to [Sir Alfred Bird, 1st Baronet](/wiki/Sir_Alfred_Bird,_1st_Baronet "Sir Alfred Bird, 1st Baronet"), a chemist and politician, who would later leave the land for creation of a [Tudor Grange grammar school](/wiki/Tudor_Grange_Academy,_Solihull "Tudor Grange Academy, Solihull") and [Tudor Grange Park](/wiki/Tudor_Grange_Park "Tudor Grange Park"). Longdon hall was eventually sold to a nearby golf course.[[34]](#cite_note-auto6-34)

In 1868, [George Muntz](/wiki/George_Muntz "George Muntz") of nearby [Umberslade](/wiki/Umberslade_Hall "Umberslade Hall"), a [campaigner for suffrage](/wiki/Chartism "Chartism") and friend of [Thomas Attwood](/wiki/Thomas_Attwood_(economist) "Thomas Attwood (economist)"), would become the owner of the manor of [Widney Manor](/wiki/Widney_Manor "Widney Manor"). He would also later buy land in [Bentley Heath](/wiki/Bentley_Heath "Bentley Heath") and develop the Solihull area of [Dorridge](/wiki/Dorridge#Muntz_family_influence "Dorridge").

In the 1870s, the Hobday family would construct Monkspath Priory. The property would later serve Solihull as the Regency Club, a [gentlemen's club](/wiki/Gentlemen%27s_club "Gentlemen's club") and banqueting complex, before becoming a hotel in the late 20th century.[[50]](#cite_note-50)

In 1870, metallurgist and inventor [James Fern Webster](/w/index.php?title=James_Fern_Webster&action=edit&redlink=1 "James Fern Webster (page does not exist)") moved to [Whitlocks End](/wiki/Dickens_Heath "Dickens Heath") on the outskirts of Solihull. While here, he discovered the process for making the extraction of [aluminium](/wiki/Aluminium "Aluminium") sufficiently cost effective for the metal to be used in the manufacture of everyday objects. Before this, aluminium was considered a precious metal, with bars of aluminium exhibited alongside the French Crown Jewels in the Paris Exhibition of 1855. In 1878, Webster was producing 100 pounds of pure Aluminium every week at his Solihull Lodge factory.[[51]](#cite_note-51)

In 1898, the Fowlers Cheese company - the oldest cheese manufacturer in England, founded 1670 - moved to its current site in Earlswood.[[52]](#cite_note-auto2-52)

In the 1890s, famed pen manufacturer [Joseph Gillott](/wiki/Joseph_Gillott "Joseph Gillott") constructed [New Berry Hall](/wiki/New_Berry_Hall "New Berry Hall") outside Catherine de Barnes. The architect of the hall was [J. A. Chatwin](/wiki/J._A._Chatwin "J. A. Chatwin") who designed the [Victoria Tower](/wiki/Victoria_Tower "Victoria Tower") in the Houses of Parliament. Around this time Chatwin also designed School House at Solihull School.

Towards the end of this era telephone and postal services came to the town.

It was said the post office in Castle Bromwich, later part of the borough of Solihull, was one of the first locations to have a telephone installed outside of London. The purported reason for this is that in 1876 Prime Minister [Benjamin Disraeli](/wiki/Benjamin_Disraeli "Benjamin Disraeli") was visiting, supposedly to court Lady Bradford at [Castle Bromwich Hall](/wiki/Castle_Bromwich_Hall "Castle Bromwich Hall"), and needed to be able to contact the government in London.[[53]](#cite_note-53)

During the Victorian era a rare fluted [pillar box](/wiki/Pillar_box "Pillar box") was installed on Dog Kennel lane. Today it is Grade II listed. The post box is designed to look like a [Doric](/wiki/Doric_order "Doric order") column.[[54]](#cite_note-54)

### 20th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Solihull&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: 20th century")]

Unlike major cities to the north, the [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution") largely passed Solihull by, along with much of the rest of rural [Warwickshire](/wiki/Warwickshire "Warwickshire") and, until the 20th century, Solihull remained a small [market town](/wiki/Market_town "Market town"). Local population growth was due to a number of factors, perhaps most significantly, the release of large tracts of land for housing development, attracting inward migration of new residents from across the United Kingdom.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/Bar_Censa%2C_Solihull_-_geograph.org.uk_-_403884.jpg/250px-Bar_Censa%2C_Solihull_-_geograph.org.uk_-_403884.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bar_Censa,_Solihull_-_geograph.org.uk_-_403884.jpg)

[The Old Council House](/wiki/Old_Council_House,_Solihull "Old Council House, Solihull")

In 1894, Solihull (including the parishes of Shirley, Baddesley Clinton, Barston, Lapworth, Balsall, Bushwood, Elmdon, Knowle, Nuthurst, Packwood and Tanworth-in-Arden) was made into the Solihull Rural District in the county of Warwickshire. In 1932, some of its rural areas were taken away when the RDC was succeeded by Solihull Urban District Council.

Solihull School continued to grow during that period. [Arthur Tolkien](/wiki/Tolkien_family#Arthur_Tolkien "Tolkien family"), father of [J. R. R. Tolkien](/wiki/J._R._R._Tolkien "J. R. R. Tolkien") (the author of *[The Lord of the Rings](/wiki/The_Lord_of_the_Rings "The Lord of the Rings")* series of books) was a pupil there during the 1860s. J. R. R. Tolkien mentioned Solihull and Solihull School in some of his letters. J. R. R. Tolkien's son [Michael](/wiki/Tolkien_family#Michael_Hilary_R._Tolkien "Tolkien family") went on to teach at Solihull School in the 1950s.[[55]](#cite_note-55) During the early 20th century, the poet [W. H. Auden](/wiki/W._H._Auden "W. H. Auden") grew up in Solihull, on Homer Road.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In 1909, Cathleen Cartland founded one of the country's first non-denominational co-educational preparatory schools - Ruckleigh School - in Solihull, many decades before others followed.

In common with most parts of the UK, Solihull and its borough was affected by the [First World War](/wiki/First_World_War "First World War"). In 1914, the [Army Remount Service](/wiki/Army_Remount_Service "Army Remount Service"), responsible for the nations [war horses](/wiki/Horses_in_World_War_I "Horses in World War I") (specifically collecting horses and dispatching them to combat areas) was headquartered in what is now the Wilson Arms in [Knowle](/wiki/Knowle,_West_Midlands "Knowle, West Midlands").[[56]](#cite_note-56) The town and borough also provided a number of Auxiliary Hospitals.[[57]](#cite_note-57) The village of [Hockley Heath](/wiki/Hockley_Heath "Hockley Heath") was bombed by a lost [Zeppelin](/wiki/Zeppelin "Zeppelin"), L 62.

In 1936, two Solihull farms were bought for the construction of a [shadow factory](/wiki/British_shadow_factories "British shadow factories") to prepare for war. The purpose of the shadow factories were to be as secret as possible, often located in rural areas, to prevent enemy bombing. After the war in 1948, this factory became the [home](/wiki/Solihull_plant "Solihull plant") of the [Rover](/wiki/Rover_Company "Rover Company") car company.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/69/Avro_652A_Anson_T21%2C_Gulf_Aviation_AN2220324.jpg/250px-Avro_652A_Anson_T21%2C_Gulf_Aviation_AN2220324.jpg)](/wiki/File:Avro_652A_Anson_T21,_Gulf_Aviation_AN2220324.jpg)

[Avro 652A Anson T21](/wiki/Avro_Anson "Avro Anson") at the Elmdon Terminal, during the celebration of the 60th anniversary of the airport, 1999

On 8 July 1939, Prime Minister [Neville Chamberlain](/wiki/Neville_Chamberlain "Neville Chamberlain") and the [Duchess of Kent](/wiki/Princess_Marina_of_Greece_and_Denmark "Princess Marina of Greece and Denmark") opened "[Elmdon Airport](/wiki/Birmingham_Airport "Birmingham Airport")" in Solihull. The Elmdon Terminal, which incorporated the airport's air traffic control tower, is famous for having been designed by [Norman and Dawbarn](/wiki/Norman_and_Dawbarn "Norman and Dawbarn") in the [Art Deco](/wiki/Art_Deco "Art Deco") style. Shortly after its opening, the airport was requisitioned for use in [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), becoming RAF Elmdon. On 8 July 1946, the aerodrome was reopened to civilian operations, although it remained under government control. In 1960, control of the airport was handed to [Birmingham City Council](/wiki/Birmingham_City_Council "Birmingham City Council"), and it was renamed "Birmingham International Airport". At that time, despite being England's second city, Birmingham was without an airport, and Elmdon Airport in Solihull was the closest. Reflecting the fact that Birmingham Airport was in Solihull and not Birmingham, [Birmingham International Rail Station](/wiki/Birmingham_International_railway_station "Birmingham International railway station") was provided to transport passengers over the 8 miles (13 km) journey to Birmingham. The airport and station are connected via [Air-Rail Link](/wiki/Air-Rail_Link "Air-Rail Link"). Amongst some Solihull residents, the airport is still affectionately known as Elmdon Airport.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

By the time [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") began, Solihull had become a reception area for evacuated children from [Coventry](/wiki/Coventry "Coventry") and the [East End of London](/wiki/East_End_of_London "East End of London"). Some [barrage balloons](/wiki/Barrage_balloon "Barrage balloon"), [smokescreen generators](/wiki/Smoke_screen "Smoke screen") and [searchlights](/wiki/Searchlight "Searchlight") were erected around Solihull in an effort to confuse any enemy bomber aircraft flying over head on their way to critical major industrial areas. A military [convalescent](/wiki/Convalescence "Convalescence") hospital was opened in Tudor Grange House, and a [British Restaurant](/wiki/British_Restaurant "British Restaurant") was opened up on Mill Lane. The town adopted the ship [HMS Vivacious](/wiki/HMS_Vivacious_(D36) "HMS Vivacious (D36)") in 1942. In preparation for the [D-Day landings](/wiki/Normandy_landings "Normandy landings"), an American Army headquarters was established on Blossomfield Road.[[58]](#cite_note-58) The town also had a [Home Guard](/wiki/Home_Guard_(United_Kingdom) "Home Guard (United Kingdom)") Battalion.[[59]](#cite_note-59)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/40/Warwickshire_emblems%2C_Ulverley_School_gates%2C_Rodney_Road%2C_Olton_-_geograph.org.uk_-_4382115.jpg/250px-Warwickshire_emblems%2C_Ulverley_School_gates%2C_Rodney_Road%2C_Olton_-_geograph.org.uk_-_4382115.jpg)](/wiki/File:Warwickshire_emblems,_Ulverley_School_gates,_Rodney_Road,_Olton_-_geograph.org.uk_-_4382115.jpg)

Warwickshire Coat of Arms at Ulverley School, showing Solihull's history as part of Warwickshire

In 1954, Queen Elizabeth II granted a Royal Charter of Incorporation, making Solihull a Municipal Borough and, ten years later, it was given the status of County Borough. Reorganisation of boundaries and council responsibilities in 1974 created the Metropolitan Borough of Solihull, by the merger of the Solihull County Borough and most of the Meriden Rural District, which forms the main rural part of the borough and county. It included Balsall Common, Barston, Berkswell, Bickenhill, Castle Bromwich, Chelmsley Wood, Elmdon, Fordbridge, Hampton in Arden, Hockley Heath, Kingshurst, Knowle, Marston Green, Meriden, Olton, Smiths Wood, Solihull, Shirley and Temple Balsall. A coat of arms was granted to the borough, with a Black Griffin taken from the arms of the Earls of Aylesford of Meriden, a Silver Fleur-de-lys comes from the Digby family associated with Coleshill and Fordbridge, and a Black Greyhound taken from the arms of the Greswolds, of Solihull town.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d0/Building_an_Icon-_Defender_2%2C000%2C000_%2819071596392%29.jpg/250px-Building_an_Icon-_Defender_2%2C000%2C000_%2819071596392%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Building_an_Icon-_Defender_2,000,000_(19071596392).jpg)

The Land Rover logo exhibits a "Solihull, England" mark

Until the early 1960s, the main high street remained much as it was in the late 19th century, with several streets of [Victorian](/wiki/Victorian_architecture "Victorian architecture") [terraced houses](/wiki/Terraced_house "Terraced house") linking High Street with Warwick Road. The construction of the central shopping area, named Mell Square, after W. Maurice Mell, the town clerk who planned the work, involved the demolition of properties in Mill Lane and Drury Lane, some of which were several hundred years old, together with the large Victorian [Congregational church](/wiki/Congregational_church "Congregational church") that had stood on the corner of Union Street and Warwick Road.

In 1966, Silhill Hall, the 13th century manor house in Solihull, was illegally demolished by the owner, Malcolm Ross, who unsuccessfully attempted to claim that a violent storm had made the structure unsafe. The site of the property is now used for housing, remembered only by the name of Silhill Hall
Road.[[60]](#cite_note-60)[[61]](#cite_note-61)[[62]](#cite_note-62)

In 1986, the Solihull borough effectively became a unitary authority, following the abolition of the County Council. The borough is administered from a building on Church Hill in Solihull town centre.

On 23 November 1981, [an F0/T1 tornado](/wiki/1981_United_Kingdom_tornado_outbreak "1981 United Kingdom tornado outbreak") touched down in nearby [Shirley](/wiki/Shirley,_West_Midlands "Shirley, West Midlands"). The tornado affected the Solihull Lodge neighbourhood, causing minor damage to Windmill Road.[[63]](#cite_note-63)[[64]](#cite_note-:0-64)

### Present day

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Solihull&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Present day")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9f/A_look_up_High_Street_in_Solihull.jpg/250px-A_look_up_High_Street_in_Solihull.jpg)](/wiki/File:A_look_up_High_Street_in_Solihull.jpg)

A look up High Street in Solihull

Despite its proximity to cities like [Birmingham](/wiki/Birmingham "Birmingham") to the northwest, Coventry to the east, [Worcester](/wiki/Worcester,_England "Worcester, England") to the southwest and the build-up of conurbations around nearby [Warwick](/wiki/Warwick "Warwick"), [Stratford-upon-Avon](/wiki/Stratford-upon-Avon "Stratford-upon-Avon"), the [Royal Town of Sutton Coldfield](/wiki/Sutton_Coldfield "Sutton Coldfield"), and [Royal Leamington Spa](/wiki/Royal_Leamington_Spa "Royal Leamington Spa"), Solihull maintains its unique and distinct identity and is bordered by greenbelt countryside to the south and east.

Solihull itself remains administered by [Solihull Metropolitan Borough Council](/wiki/Solihull_Metropolitan_Borough_Council "Solihull Metropolitan Borough Council"), effectively a [unitary authority](/wiki/Unitary_authority "Unitary authority") providing the majority of local government services. Regional administrative functions such as policing, fire and public transport are organised through the [West Midlands](/wiki/West_Midlands_(county) "West Midlands (county)") administrative division and combined authority.

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Solihull&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Geography")]

Solihull is located in the historic [Forest of Arden](/wiki/Forest_of_Arden "Forest of Arden") and on the banks of the [River Blythe](/wiki/River_Blythe "River Blythe") 127 m (416 ft) above sea level.[[65]](#cite_note-65) The town, and most of the borough is within the [West Midlands Green Belt](/wiki/West_Midlands_Green_Belt "West Midlands Green Belt").

The town is 8 miles (13 km) southeast of Birmingham, 14 miles (21 km) west of [Coventry](/wiki/Coventry "Coventry"), 19 miles (31 km) northwest of [Warwick](/wiki/Warwick "Warwick") and 110 miles (180 km) northwest of [London](/wiki/London "London").[[66]](#cite_note-66)

The village of [Meriden](/wiki/Meriden,_West_Midlands "Meriden, West Midlands") in the Borough of Solihull was historically regarded as the [centre of England](/wiki/Centre_points_of_the_United_Kingdom#England "Centre points of the United Kingdom") and marked the dividing line between [England's North and South](/wiki/North%E2%80%93South_divide_in_England "North–South divide in England"). A [listed](/wiki/Listed_building "Listed building") stone monument commemorating Meriden's status as 'Centre of England' sits on the village green. However, this was discovered to be inaccurate in the 1920s, when the centre of England was in fact calculated to be in [Leicestershire](/wiki/Leicestershire "Leicestershire").[[67]](#cite_note-67)

## Governance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Solihull&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Governance")]

### Local Government Level

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Solihull&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Local Government Level")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/07/Urbs_in_Rure_-_geograph.org.uk_-_5828502.jpg/250px-Urbs_in_Rure_-_geograph.org.uk_-_5828502.jpg)](/wiki/File:Urbs_in_Rure_-_geograph.org.uk_-_5828502.jpg)

[Coat of Arms](/wiki/Coat_of_arms "Coat of arms") of the former county borough of Solihull commemorating prominent Manorial Lords and Stewards of the town: Argent within two barrulets (for Throckmorton) between in chief as many pierced mullets (for Oddingsells) and in base a Saxon crown (for Princess Cristina) all Gules, a greyhound courant Sable (for Greswold).

Due to its growth, Solihull was promoted from an [urban district](/wiki/Urban_district_(Great_Britain_and_Ireland) "Urban district (Great Britain and Ireland)") to a [municipal borough](/wiki/Municipal_borough "Municipal borough"), the honour being bestowed by [Princess Margaret](/wiki/Princess_Margaret,_Countess_of_Snowdon "Princess Margaret, Countess of Snowdon"), who waved from the balcony of the [Old Council House](/wiki/Old_Council_House,_Solihull "Old Council House, Solihull") in March 1954.[[68]](#cite_note-68)

[Queen Elizabeth II](/wiki/Elizabeth_II "Elizabeth II") opened the new civic hall in the town in May 1962.[[69]](#cite_note-69) The town became a [county borough](/wiki/County_borough "County borough") in 1964. In 1974, the Solihull county borough was merged with the rural district surrounding [Meriden](/wiki/Meriden,_West_Midlands "Meriden, West Midlands") to form the [Metropolitan Borough of Solihull](/wiki/Metropolitan_Borough_of_Solihull "Metropolitan Borough of Solihull"). This also includes the districts known as [Shirley](/wiki/Shirley,_West_Midlands "Shirley, West Midlands"), [Knowle](/wiki/Knowle,_West_Midlands "Knowle, West Midlands"), [Dorridge](/wiki/Dorridge "Dorridge"), [Balsall Common](/wiki/Balsall_Common "Balsall Common"), [Castle Bromwich](/wiki/Castle_Bromwich "Castle Bromwich") and [Chelmsley Wood](/wiki/Chelmsley_Wood "Chelmsley Wood").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/39/Brueton_Gardens_Clock.JPG/250px-Brueton_Gardens_Clock.JPG)](/wiki/File:Brueton_Gardens_Clock.JPG)

Solihull clocktower, erected to mark the elevation of Solihull to a County Borough in 1964

There are now 17 wards in Solihull;[[70]](#cite_note-cstructure-70) [Olton](/wiki/Olton "Olton"), [Knowle](/wiki/Knowle,_West_Midlands "Knowle, West Midlands"), [Dorridge](/wiki/Dorridge "Dorridge"), [Silhill](/wiki/Silhill "Silhill"), Blythe, [Meriden](/wiki/Meriden,_West_Midlands "Meriden, West Midlands"), [Elmdon](/wiki/Elmdon,_West_Midlands "Elmdon, West Midlands"), [Lyndon](/wiki/Lyndon,_West_Midlands "Lyndon, West Midlands"), [Smith's Wood](/wiki/Smith%27s_Wood "Smith's Wood"), [Chelmsley Wood](/wiki/Chelmsley_Wood "Chelmsley Wood"), [Hockley Heath](/wiki/Hockley_Heath "Hockley Heath"), St. Alphege, Shirley West, Shirley East, Shirley South, [Kingshurst](/wiki/Kingshurst "Kingshurst") & [Fordbridge](/wiki/Fordbridge "Fordbridge"), [Castle Bromwich](/wiki/Castle_Bromwich "Castle Bromwich") and [Bickenhill](/wiki/Bickenhill "Bickenhill").[[71]](#cite_note-71) Each ward is represented by three councillors at Solihull Metropolitan Borough Council, making a total of 51 councillors.[[70]](#cite_note-cstructure-70) The mayor is elected by the council and is currently (2022) Ken Meeson of the [Conservative Party](/wiki/Conservative_Party_(UK) "Conservative Party (UK)").

There are [parish councils](/wiki/Parish_council_(England) "Parish council (England)") serving villages across the wider borough.

### Regional and National Government Level

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Solihull&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Regional and National Government Level")]

Regionally, Solihull comes under the [West Midlands Combined Authority](/wiki/West_Midlands_Combined_Authority "West Midlands Combined Authority"), and therefore also takes part in elections for the [Mayor of the West Midlands](/wiki/Mayor_of_the_West_Midlands "Mayor of the West Midlands") and the [West Midlands Police and Crime Commissioner](/wiki/West_Midlands_Police_and_Crime_Commissioner "West Midlands Police and Crime Commissioner").[[72]](#cite_note-72)[[73]](#cite_note-73)

The [member of parliament](/wiki/Member_of_parliament "Member of parliament") for the [Solihull West and Shirley](/wiki/Solihull_West_and_Shirley "Solihull West and Shirley") constituency is [Conservative](/wiki/UK_Conservative_Party "UK Conservative Party") [Dr Neil Shastri-Hurst](/wiki/Neil_Shastri-Hurst "Neil Shastri-Hurst"), who won his seat in 2024 after the constituency was created in 2023. The seat is one of three parliamentary seats within the Borough of Solihull, the others being [Meriden & Solihull East Constituency](/wiki/Meriden_and_Solihull_East_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Meriden and Solihull East (UK Parliament constituency)"), named for the village of [Meriden](/wiki/Meriden,_West_Midlands "Meriden, West Midlands") within the [Meriden Gap](/wiki/Meriden_Gap "Meriden Gap") and the third is the [Birmingham Hodge Hill and Solihull North](/wiki/Birmingham_Hodge_Hill_and_Solihull_North_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Birmingham Hodge Hill and Solihull North (UK Parliament constituency)") Constituency.

### Traditions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Solihull&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Traditions")]

Traditionally the Mayor of Solihull is driven in a Land Rover - usually a Range Rover - with the licence plate 'Sol 1', reflecting the towns connection to the Land Rover vehicle manufacturer.[[74]](#cite_note-74)

Although the town and borough of Solihull are in the [West Midlands](/wiki/West_Midlands_(county) "West Midlands (county)") metropolitan county, Solihull is in the [historic county](/wiki/Historic_counties_of_England "Historic counties of England") of [Warwickshire](/wiki/Warwickshire "Warwickshire"). This means the town can fly the [flag of Warwickshire](/wiki/Flag_of_Warwickshire "Flag of Warwickshire") and celebrate ["Warwickshire Day"](/wiki/List_of_county_days_in_the_United_Kingdom "List of county days in the United Kingdom"), when an official date is agreed upon.[[75]](#cite_note-75)[[76]](#cite_note-76) Some local support has been recorded to return the borough entirely to Warwickshire, such as when Bath was returned to Somerset after the abolition of the County of Avon.[[77]](#cite_note-77)

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Solihull&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Education")]

See also: [List of schools in Solihull](/wiki/List_of_schools_in_Solihull "List of schools in Solihull")

### Higher education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Solihull&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Higher education")]

Solihull has no university, but there are seven universities within 16 mi (26 km) of the town in the surrounding cities. However, [Solihull College](/wiki/Solihull_College "Solihull College") **& University Centre**, formerly known as the **Solihull College of Technology**, offers several foundation degree and full degree courses, particularly in technical subject areas such as computer sciences and engineering. These courses are offered through the college's partner, the [University of Warwick](/wiki/University_of_Warwick "University of Warwick"). As yet the college has not applied to attain [university college](/wiki/University_college "University college") status.

### Further education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Solihull&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Further education")]

There is also a [sixth form college](/wiki/Sixth_form_college "Sixth form college") located on the outskirts of the town centre. This is known as [Solihull Sixth Form College](/wiki/The_Sixth_Form_College,_Solihull "The Sixth Form College, Solihull").

### Independent schools

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Solihull&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Independent schools")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0a/Solihull_School_Chatwin.jpg/250px-Solihull_School_Chatwin.jpg)](/wiki/File:Solihull_School_Chatwin.jpg)

Solihull School

[Solihull School](/wiki/Solihull_School "Solihull School") is an independent school and is located on Warwick Road near the centre of the town. It was founded in 1560 and celebrated its 450th anniversary in 2010. It merged with independent girls school St Martins School in 2020.[[78]](#cite_note-78)

[Ruckleigh School](/w/index.php?title=Ruckleigh_School&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ruckleigh School (page does not exist)") is an independent preparatory school founded in 1909 by Cathleen Cartland and is notable for being a nondenominational co-educational school many decades [before any others followed](/wiki/Mixed-sex_education#Schools "Mixed-sex education"). Another co-education preparatory school called [Eversfield Preparatory School](/w/index.php?title=Eversfield_Preparatory_School&action=edit&redlink=1 "Eversfield Preparatory School (page does not exist)"), founded in 1931 by Cyril Denney, is also located in the town.

### State-funded schools

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Solihull&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: State-funded schools")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/32/Tudor_Grange_Academy%2C_Dingle_Lane_-_geograph.org.uk_-_3185037.jpg/250px-Tudor_Grange_Academy%2C_Dingle_Lane_-_geograph.org.uk_-_3185037.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tudor_Grange_Academy,_Dingle_Lane_-_geograph.org.uk_-_3185037.jpg)

Tudor Grange Academy

The town centre is also well served by some high performing [state schools](/wiki/State-funded_schools_(England) "State-funded schools (England)"). Tudor Grange house, a now grade II listed property, was completed in Solihull in 1887 for Alfred Lovekin, a silversmith, who later sold the estate to Sir Alfred Bird, the owner of Bird's Custard then produced at the [Birmingham Custard Factory](/wiki/Custard_Factory "Custard Factory"). The Bird family bequeathed the house and estate to the people of Solihull for the construction of a 'Tudor Grange' [grammar school](/wiki/Grammar_school "Grammar school") around the turn of the century. After [Tudor Grange grammar school](/wiki/Tudor_Grange_Academy,_Solihull "Tudor Grange Academy, Solihull") had been established on this site, other parcels of the estate were passed on by the school for other neighbouring schools, colleges and parkland.[[79]](#cite_note-79) Tudor Grange Academy and Solihull School continue to enjoy a friendly sporting rivalry centred around rugby union. Solihull's state schools, Tudor Grange Academy and [Arden Academy](/wiki/Arden_Academy "Arden Academy"), regularly feature in good school guides, such as "the *[Tatler](/wiki/Tatler "Tatler")* guide to the best state secondary schools".[[80]](#cite_note-80)

Solihull had a 'Wave 1' proposal of the [Building Schools for the Future](/wiki/Building_Schools_for_the_Future "Building Schools for the Future") investment programme approved. They were awarded over £80 million to transform six schools in the north of the borough in December 2004. As a result of the funding, there will be six new schools constructed within seven years. The school curriculum will be redesigned as well as a further £6 million investment in managed ICT services. The six schools to be rebuilt are [Park Hall Academy](/wiki/Park_Hall_Academy "Park Hall Academy"), [Smith's Wood Academy](/wiki/Smith%27s_Wood_Academy "Smith's Wood Academy"), [Archbishop Grimshaw](/wiki/Archbishop_Grimshaw_School "Archbishop Grimshaw School"), [Lanchester Special School](/w/index.php?title=Lanchester_Special_School&action=edit&redlink=1 "Lanchester Special School (page does not exist)"), [Forest Oak School](/w/index.php?title=Forest_Oak_School&action=edit&redlink=1 "Forest Oak School (page does not exist)") and [Merstone school](/w/index.php?title=Merstone_school&action=edit&redlink=1 "Merstone school (page does not exist)"). Forest Oak and Merstone have been already rebuilt on one site. Lanchester, Park Hall and Smith's Wood have been built by BAM PPP, under '[Private Finance Initiative](/wiki/Private_Finance_Initiative "Private Finance Initiative")'. Archbishop Grimshaw has been built by BAM PPP under a traditional contract.[[81]](#cite_note-81)

## Transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Solihull&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Transport")]

### Road

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Solihull&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Road")]

A number of main roads pass through Solihull including the [A41 Birmingham to Warwick](/wiki/A41_road "A41 road") road and the [A34](/wiki/A34_road_(England) "A34 road (England)") Birmingham to [Stratford](/wiki/Stratford_upon_Avon "Stratford upon Avon") road: the A34 becomes the commercial centre of Shirley, making for a busy town-centre feel along the main road.

The [M42](/wiki/M42_motorway "M42 motorway") and the [M40](/wiki/M40_motorway "M40 motorway") both pass through the outskirts of the borough of Solihull, providing very rapid links to [Oxford](/wiki/Oxford "Oxford") and [London](/wiki/London "London") in the South East, and to the rest of the motorway network surrounding the [West Midlands](/wiki/West_Midlands_(region) "West Midlands (region)") region. The M42 opens up to the [M5](/wiki/M5_motorway "M5 motorway"), providing links to [Cheltenham](/wiki/Cheltenham "Cheltenham"), the [West Country](/wiki/West_Country "West Country") and [South West England](/wiki/South_West_England "South West England").

### Rail

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Solihull&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Rail")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/67/2013_at_Solihull_station_-_main_entrance.jpg/250px-2013_at_Solihull_station_-_main_entrance.jpg)](/wiki/File:2013_at_Solihull_station_-_main_entrance.jpg)

Solihull station

[Solihull railway station](/wiki/Solihull_railway_station "Solihull railway station") is on the former [Great Western Railway](/wiki/Great_Western_Railway "Great Western Railway") line to [London Paddington](/wiki/Paddington_railway_station "Paddington railway station") although trains now run along the [Chiltern Main Line](/wiki/Chiltern_Main_Line "Chiltern Main Line") terminating at [London Marylebone](/wiki/Marylebone_station "Marylebone station").

Other railway links are provided on the [West Coast Main Line](/wiki/West_Coast_Main_Line "West Coast Main Line"), as [Birmingham International railway station](/wiki/Birmingham_International_railway_station "Birmingham International railway station") lies within the borough's boundaries and offers frequent express connections to London. Express train services through Solihull are now run by [Chiltern Railways](/wiki/Chiltern_Railways "Chiltern Railways") and local services by [West Midlands Railway](/wiki/West_Midlands_Railway "West Midlands Railway"). Cross Country trains pass through the station but no longer stop here.

Since 2023 the luxury train Midland Pullman - an [HST](/wiki/InterCity_125 "InterCity 125") set operated by [Locomotive Services Ltd](/wiki/Locomotive_Services_Limited "Locomotive Services Limited") repainted in replica Blue Pullman livery - runs tours from Solihull along the [Settle to Carlisle](/wiki/Settle%E2%80%93Carlisle_line "Settle–Carlisle line") railway.[[82]](#cite_note-82)

Solihull will be served by a [HS2](/wiki/High_Speed_2 "High Speed 2") interchange station if the project is approved, putting London just 38 minutes away by train.[[83]](#cite_note-83)[[84]](#cite_note-84)

### Bus

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Solihull&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Bus")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/21/Diamond_30401_on_Route_82%2C_Solihull_%2821516685963%29.jpg/250px-Diamond_30401_on_Route_82%2C_Solihull_%2821516685963%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Diamond_30401_on_Route_82,_Solihull_(21516685963).jpg)

A [Mercedes-Benz Citaro](/wiki/Mercedes-Benz_Citaro "Mercedes-Benz Citaro") bus in *Signature* livery in Solihull

Local bus services are provided largely by [National Express West Midlands](/wiki/National_Express_West_Midlands "National Express West Midlands") from their [Yardley Wood](/wiki/Yardley_Wood "Yardley Wood") and [Acocks Green](/wiki/Acocks_Green "Acocks Green") depots in south and southeast Birmingham respectively. Other services are provided by [Landflight](/w/index.php?title=Landflight&action=edit&redlink=1 "Landflight (page does not exist)") who operate a number of local services and [Diamond West Midlands](/wiki/Diamond_West_Midlands "Diamond West Midlands") which originally included service X20 between [Coventry](/wiki/Coventry "Coventry") and [Stratford-upon-Avon](/wiki/Stratford-upon-Avon "Stratford-upon-Avon"), one of several services taken over with the bus operations of Johnsons. Until 2019 Diamond buses operated the *Signature* brand of buses in Solihull. The Diamond buses operating in Solihull were fitted with leather seats and wireless internet, and used the cleanest bus engines in Europe.[[85]](#cite_note-85) The Diamond buses have since moved elsewhere.

Due to a review of services in Warwickshire by Diamond, service X20 was split into two parts from 13 August 2022 with [Stagecoach](/wiki/Stagecoach "Stagecoach") operating the Stratford to Solihull section daily. The remaining section between Solihull and Coventry continued to be operated by Diamond Mon-Sat until 28 August 2022 when this section also began being operated by [Stagecoach](/wiki/Stagecoach_in_Warwickshire "Stagecoach in Warwickshire") as service 82. Stagecoach also began operating services 87 & 88 on the same date.

In preparation for the [2022 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/2022_Commonwealth_Games "2022 Commonwealth Games") which will be held across the West Midlands and Warwickshire, [Transport for West Midlands](/wiki/Transport_for_West_Midlands "Transport for West Midlands") is constructing a new [Bus Rapid Transit](/wiki/Bus_rapid_transit "Bus rapid transit") network of tram style buses that will form critical infrastructure ahead of the games. The new network, known as Sprint, will utilise dedicated lanes and zero-emission tram style buses which will provide off-board ticketing, multiple-door boarding, wheelchair and pushchair access, free Wi-Fi, and air conditioning as well as on-board audio visual announcements and travel information. The network will connect Solihull town centre, Birmingham Airport, the National Exhibition Centre and future HS2 Interchange (all in Solihull) with Birmingham City Centre, and other parts of the region, such as the Royal Town of Sutton Coldfield. Any green space lost as part of Sprint's proposals will be replaced according to the local highway authority tree replacement policy. Work commenced on the network in March 2021.[[86]](#cite_note-86)[[87]](#cite_note-87)[[88]](#cite_note-88)[[89]](#cite_note-89)

### Canal

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Solihull&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Canal")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bf/Hotel_butty_negotiating_Shirley_Drawbridge_near_Solihull_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1724211.jpg/250px-Hotel_butty_negotiating_Shirley_Drawbridge_near_Solihull_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1724211.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hotel_butty_negotiating_Shirley_Drawbridge_near_Solihull_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1724211.jpg)

A [Narrowboat](/wiki/Narrowboat "Narrowboat") on the Canal in Shirley

The [Grand Union Canal](/wiki/Grand_Union_Canal "Grand Union Canal") passes across Solihull, coming within 1 mi (1.6 km) of the town centre and linking the town to the [River Thames](/wiki/River_Thames "River Thames") in [London](/wiki/London "London"). There are a flight of five locks at Knowle added in the 1930s which are wide enough to allow narrowboats to navigate together, side by side.[[90]](#cite_note-90)

The [Stratford-upon-Avon Canal](/wiki/Stratford-upon-Avon_Canal "Stratford-upon-Avon Canal") passes through Hockley Heath in the Borough of Solihull. Hockley Wharf (now a [public house](/wiki/Pub "Pub")) served the rest of the surrounding Solihull area and beyond. Non-perishable goods were unloaded there, including timber, lime, coke, coal, cement, bricks and salt. The last commercial load was brought into the wharf on Christmas Eve, 1929.[[91]](#cite_note-91)

### Bicycle

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Solihull&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Bicycle")]

Bicycling in Solihull is popular, and as of March 2021 it was announced that Solihull would benefit from a cycle hire scheme, where residents and visitors would be able to hire bikes from an app on their phone. Three free to use cycle repair stations are being installed across the borough, and new cycle lanes added.[[92]](#cite_note-92)

### Trams

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Solihull&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Trams")]

In 2024, work will commence on an extension to the Transport for West Midlands [West Midlands Metro](/wiki/West_Midlands_Metro "West Midlands Metro") tram network down to Solihull. The extension will link up to Birmingham Airport, the National Exhibition Centre and future HS2 Interchange with the wider network, before continuing on through the region, going as far south as the [University of Warwick](/wiki/University_of_Warwick "University of Warwick").[[93]](#cite_note-93)[[94]](#cite_note-94)

### Air

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Solihull&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Air")]

[Birmingham Airport](/wiki/Birmingham_Airport "Birmingham Airport") is located in the Elmdon part of Solihull and was known as Elmdon Airport until control passed from the government to Birmingham City Council in the 1960s, as Birmingham itself was without an airport. Elmdon Airport was 8 miles from Birmingham, but still closer than any other contender. The airport is connected to the NEC and Birmingham International Station via the [Air-Rail Link](/wiki/Air-Rail_Link "Air-Rail Link") [APM](/wiki/People_mover "People mover").

## Demographics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Solihull&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Demographics")]

At the [2021 census](/wiki/United_Kingdom_census,_2021 "United Kingdom census, 2021"), there were 107,728 residents in the built-up area of Solihull, up from 104,905 in the 2011 census, and 100,369 at the 2001 census.[[95]](#cite_note-SolihullBUA-95)

In terms of ethnicity in 2021:[[95]](#cite_note-SolihullBUA-95)

* 76.4% of Solihull residents were [White](/wiki/White_British "White British")
* 17% were [Asian](/wiki/British_Asian "British Asian")
* 1.5% were [Black](/wiki/Black_British "Black British")
* 2.8% were [Mixed](/wiki/Mixed_(United_Kingdom_ethnicity_category) "Mixed (United Kingdom ethnicity category)").
* 1.6% were from another ethnic group.

In terms of religion, 52.4% of Solihull residents identified as [Christian](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity"), 29.6% said they had [no religion](/wiki/Irreligion "Irreligion"), 8.6% were [Muslim](/wiki/Islam "Islam"), 4.9% were [Hindu](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism"), 3.6% were [Sikh](/wiki/Sikhism "Sikhism"), 0.3% were [Buddhists](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism"), 0.2% were [Jewish](/wiki/Judaism "Judaism"), and 0.4% were from another religion.[[95]](#cite_note-SolihullBUA-95)

### Hong Kong community

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Solihull&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Hong Kong community")]

By 2025, the community had about 4,500-5,000 people with [Hong Kong origins](/wiki/Hong_Kongers_in_the_United_Kingdom "Hong Kongers in the United Kingdom"). The [2020 Hong Kong national security law](/wiki/2020_Hong_Kong_national_security_law "2020 Hong Kong national security law") was a factor that pushed them out of Hong Kong. By 2025, Solihull had Hong Kong-style food establishments.[[96]](#cite_note-96)

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Solihull&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Economy")]

### Retail

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Solihull&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Retail")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b9/Touchwood_Solihull_Park.jpg/250px-Touchwood_Solihull_Park.jpg)](/wiki/File:Touchwood_Solihull_Park.jpg)

Touchwood, Solihull

Solihull offers a variety of shopping facilities. In recent years, the town has undergone much development, and its High Street has been pedestrianised since 1994. On 2 July 2002, a large new shopping centre, [Touchwood](/wiki/Touchwood,_Solihull "Touchwood, Solihull"), was opened by [Queen Elizabeth II](/wiki/Elizabeth_II "Elizabeth II"). The [Crown Estate](/wiki/Crown_Estate "Crown Estate") has an equity interest in the shopping centre. In 2014 the town was listed as one of the top five destinations for shopping in the UK.[[97]](#cite_note-97) In addition to the Touchwood shopping centre it also has an open-air 1960s-style shopping centre called Mell Square which was constructed following the demolition of several terraces of Victorian houses and the original Solihull [Congregational Church](/wiki/Congregational_church "Congregational church"). When Mell Square was first opened it was known for its large block of fountains, which were switched off and removed in the late 80s. In recent years some residents have called for the fountains to be returned.[[98]](#cite_note-98) Luxury vehicle sales also make up some of the Solihull economy, with a [dealership](/wiki/Car_dealership "Car dealership") for [Rolls-Royce](/wiki/Rolls-Royce_Motor_Cars "Rolls-Royce Motor Cars"), and other luxury car marques, present in the town.

### Industry

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Solihull&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Industry")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/59/1m_discoverybuild_solihull_21_%286948761579%29.jpg/250px-1m_discoverybuild_solihull_21_%286948761579%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:1m_discoverybuild_solihull_21_(6948761579).jpg)

The Land Rover Solihull Plant

Solihull is the home of the four-wheel-drive car manufacturer [Land Rover](/wiki/Land_Rover "Land Rover"). Its main [production plant](/wiki/Solihull_plant "Solihull plant") is situated east of the Lode Heath district, along with a range of other major companies. Engineering and professional services firm [Arup](/wiki/Arup_Group "Arup Group") have a large presence in Solihull.[[99]](#cite_note-99) Fitness apparel manufacturer [Gymshark](/wiki/Gymshark "Gymshark") has been based in Solihull since 2012 and women's fashion and lingerie brand [Lounge (company)](/wiki/Lounge_(company) "Lounge (company)") moved their headquarters to Solihull in 2021.

The Solihull village of [Meriden](/wiki/Meriden,_West_Midlands "Meriden, West Midlands") was the famous home of the [Triumph motorbike factory](/wiki/Triumph_Engineering "Triumph Engineering") from 1942 to 1983. In January 2021 motorcycle company [Norton](/wiki/Norton_Motorcycle_Company "Norton Motorcycle Company") announced they would be investing in a technologically advanced production site and headquarters in Solihull.[[100]](#cite_note-100) The [Lucas](/wiki/Lucas_Industries "Lucas Industries") company, which supplied lighting and electric components to the motorcycle and aerospace industries was also headquartered in Solihull for many years, with a research facility in Shirley,[[101]](#cite_note-101) now a housing development called Lucas Green.

### Food and beverage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Solihull&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Food and beverage")]

Food and drink production and hospitality also make up part of the local Solihull economy. [Earlswood](/wiki/Earlswood,_Warwickshire "Earlswood, Warwickshire") in the Solihull borough is home to Fowlers, established since 1670, it is the oldest cheese-making business in England. The company still makes all its cheese by hand.[[52]](#cite_note-auto2-52) In Catherine de Barnes is the Silhill Brewery. The former home of retail [bakers](/wiki/Baker "Baker") [Three Cooks](/wiki/Three_Cooks "Three Cooks") was based in Solihull, and after it was brought out of administration in 2006, the new company [Cooks the Bakery](/wiki/Cooks_the_Bakery "Cooks the Bakery") retains its HQ in Solihull. The [pub](/wiki/Public_house "Public house") company [Enterprise Inns](/wiki/Enterprise_Inns "Enterprise Inns") is also headquartered in Solihull. The [Grenade Protein Bar](/w/index.php?title=Grenade_Protein_Bar&action=edit&redlink=1 "Grenade Protein Bar (page does not exist)") company, valued at £200m in 2021, is based in Solihull, having been started by a couple from Solihull in 2010.[[102]](#cite_note-102)

There are a number of restaurants and eateries in Solihull, including the [Michelin starred](/wiki/Michelin_Guide "Michelin Guide") [Peel's Restaurant](/wiki/Peel%27s_Restaurant "Peel's Restaurant").

### Service sector

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Solihull&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Service sector")]

Financial services make up some of the Solihull economy. Personal loan provider [Paragon](/wiki/Paragon_Banking_Group "Paragon Banking Group"), and retail and commercial bank [Secure Trust Bank](/wiki/Secure_Trust_Bank "Secure Trust Bank") are both based in Solihull.
