The [National Exhibition Centre](/wiki/National_Exhibition_Centre "National Exhibition Centre") is within the borough of Solihull, which hosts a number of national trade shows, such as [The Horse of the Year Show](/wiki/Horse_of_the_Year_Show "Horse of the Year Show") and [The Crufts International Dog Show](/wiki/Crufts "Crufts"). The [Blythe Valley Business Park](/wiki/Blythe_Valley_Park "Blythe Valley Park") is also both within the borough of Solihull. Both have excellent links to Birmingham Airport and the [M42](/wiki/M42_motorway "M42 motorway"), [M40](/wiki/M40_motorway "M40 motorway") and [M6](/wiki/M6_motorway "M6 motorway") motorways.

### Third sector and charity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Solihull&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Third sector and charity")]

A number of regional and national charities are based in, or have offices in, Solihull. [Royal Star and Garter Homes](/wiki/Royal_Star_%26_Garter_Homes "Royal Star & Garter Homes") a charity founded in 1916 to provide care for [ex-Service people](/wiki/Veteran "Veteran") and their partners who live with disability or dementia, operates one of its three state of the art care homes in Solihull.[[103]](#cite_note-103)

## Culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Solihull&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Culture")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/16/Statue_of_a_Horse_and_Horse_Tamer_in_Malvern_Park%2C_Solihull_-_2010.JPG/250px-Statue_of_a_Horse_and_Horse_Tamer_in_Malvern_Park%2C_Solihull_-_2010.JPG)](/wiki/File:Statue_of_a_Horse_and_Horse_Tamer_in_Malvern_Park,_Solihull_-_2010.JPG)

Statue of a Horse and Tamer in Malvern Park, Solihull, by artist [Sir Joseph Edgar Boehm](/wiki/Joseph_Edgar_Boehm "Joseph Edgar Boehm"). Donated to the town by Captain Oliver Bird, High Sheriff of Warwickshire.

### Screen and stage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Solihull&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Screen and stage")]

Solihull is home to the Core Theatre, part of the Solihull Arts Complex. The centre of Solihull has a *[Cineworld](/wiki/Cineworld "Cineworld")* cinema. There is another Cineworld cinema with an [IMAX](/wiki/IMAX "IMAX") screen in the [Resorts World shopping centre](/wiki/Resorts_World_Birmingham "Resorts World Birmingham") in the Borough of Solihull.

### Music and dance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Solihull&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Music and dance")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/39/Genting_Arena.jpg/250px-Genting_Arena.jpg)](/wiki/File:Genting_Arena.jpg)

BP Pulse Live under old branding

In Solihull is the [bp pulse LIVE](/wiki/Bp_pulse_LIVE "Bp pulse LIVE") (used to be called LG Arena, Genting Arena, and Resorts World Arena), a multipurpose indoor arena with a capacity of 15,685 opened in 1980 with a concert by [Queen](/wiki/Queen_(band) "Queen (band)"). A number of internationally renowned acts have performed there in recent years, including [Lady Gaga](/wiki/Lady_Gaga "Lady Gaga") and [Adele](/wiki/Adele "Adele"). In 2019, Resorts World Arena, as it was known at the time had the fifth highest ticket sales of an arena venue in the United Kingdom.[[104]](#cite_note-104)

Solihull has a symphony orchestra founded in 1990.[[105]](#cite_note-105)

The town has a professional [ballet](/wiki/Ballet "Ballet") school, which teaches according to the [International Dance Teachers Association](/wiki/International_Dance_Teachers_Association "International Dance Teachers Association") syllabus.[[106]](#cite_note-106)

The town has a troop of [mixed border](/wiki/Border_Morris "Border Morris") [Morris dancers](/wiki/Morris_dance "Morris dance") called Silhill Morris.[[107]](#cite_note-107)

### Art and museums

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Solihull&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Art and museums")]

Solihull is home to a [large number of public sculptures and art works](/wiki/List_of_public_art_in_Solihull "List of public art in Solihull"). There are also a number of museums, galleries and historic properties open to the public.

Museums and galleries

The Solihull Arts Complex includes the Courtyard Gallery and Art Space, an exhibition space and gallery. The town also has a number of private art galleries.

Reflecting the towns British motorcycle heritage, as home to the [Triumph Motorcycle](/wiki/Triumph_Motorcycles_Ltd "Triumph Motorcycles Ltd") company for many years, Solihull also has the [National Motorcycle Museum](/wiki/National_Motorcycle_Museum_(UK) "National Motorcycle Museum (UK)"). The museum was founded in 1984 and is affiliated to the [British Motorcycle Charitable Trust](/wiki/British_Motorcycle_Charitable_Trust "British Motorcycle Charitable Trust"). The museum has become the largest collection of British motorcycles in the world, with over 250,000 visitors a year.[[108]](#cite_note-108)

Heritage and historic attractions

[Berry Mound](/wiki/Berry_Mound "Berry Mound") is the site of an Iron Age hill fort dating back to the 1st century BC in Shirely, Solihull, on the Worcestershire border.[[109]](#cite_note-109)

[Hobs Moat](/wiki/Hobs_Moat "Hobs Moat") in Olton, Solihull, is the site of the ruined 12th-century castle of the Lords of the Manor of Solihull. Today it is a [scheduled ancient monument](/wiki/Scheduled_monument "Scheduled monument") and [Local nature reserve](/wiki/Local_nature_reserve "Local nature reserve"). The ruin itself is now below ground level, but the moated earthworks remain above the ground level.[[110]](#cite_note-110)

[Baddesley Clinton](/wiki/Baddesley_Clinton "Baddesley Clinton") is a 13th-century [Grade I Listed](/wiki/Listed_building#Grade_I "Listed building") moated manor house in the village of [Baddesley Clinton](/wiki/Baddesley_Clinton_(village) "Baddesley Clinton (village)") on the border of the borough of Solihull managed by the [National Trust](/wiki/National_Trust "National Trust").

[Packwood House](/wiki/Packwood_House "Packwood House") is a Grade I listed Tudor manor house on the outskirts of the borough of Solihull managed by the [National Trust](/wiki/National_Trust "National Trust"). It was home to the Featherston family, who would later marry into the Dilke family of nearby [Maxstoke Castle](/wiki/Maxstoke_Castle "Maxstoke Castle"). They also provided the first usher at Solihull School, where a house is named Featherston.[[111]](#cite_note-auto-111) The yew tree topiary garden of Packwood House is also famous in its own right, and is supposed to symbolise the Sermon on the Mount. Packwood church, known as St Giles, was the location of the marriage of the parents of [Samuel Johnson](/wiki/Samuel_Johnson "Samuel Johnson"), author of the first dictionary. Samuel's mother, Sara Ford, was from Packwood and St Giles was her parish church.[[111]](#cite_note-auto-111)

[Berkswell Mill](/wiki/Balsall_Common_Mill,_Berkswell "Balsall Common Mill, Berkswell") is a historic windmill in the borough of Solihull which is occasionally open to the public.[[112]](#cite_note-112)

There is a historic miniature steam railway founded in 1936 in [Illshaw Heath](/wiki/Cheswick_Green "Cheswick Green") in the borough of Solihull.[[113]](#cite_note-113)

## Communal facilities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Solihull&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: Communal facilities")]

### Parks and local nature reserves

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Solihull&action=edit&section=46 "Edit section: Parks and local nature reserves")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7e/The_Lake%2C_Brueton_Park%2C_Solihull_-_geograph.org.uk_-_26515.jpg/250px-The_Lake%2C_Brueton_Park%2C_Solihull_-_geograph.org.uk_-_26515.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Lake,_Brueton_Park,_Solihull_-_geograph.org.uk_-_26515.jpg)

The Lake, [Brueton Park](/wiki/Malvern_%26_Brueton_Park "Malvern & Brueton Park"), Solihull

Solihull has a number of [parks](/wiki/Park "Park") and [local nature reserves](/wiki/Local_nature_reserve "Local nature reserve"), including the UK's first dedicated [hedgehog](/wiki/Hedgehog "Hedgehog") conservation area.[[116]](#cite_note-116)

#### Town centre

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Solihull&action=edit&section=47 "Edit section: Town centre")]

The nearest parks to the town centre are Malvern and Brueton Parks. They are interlinked and cover a total area of about 130 acres (0.53 km2). Brueton Park used to be part of the grounds of Malvern Hall, which dates back to about 1690. [Tudor Grange Park](/wiki/Tudor_Grange_Park "Tudor Grange Park") is also close to the town centre. The [River Blythe](/wiki/River_Blythe "River Blythe"), a headwater [tributary](/wiki/Tributary "Tributary") of the [River Trent](/wiki/River_Trent "River Trent"), passes through parts of Solihull including Malvern and Tudor Grange Parks. [Hillfield Park](/wiki/Hillfield_Park "Hillfield Park") is on the outskirts of the town centre.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/07/Kiosk%2C_Brueton_Park%2C_Solihull_-_geograph.org.uk_-_3935079.jpg/250px-Kiosk%2C_Brueton_Park%2C_Solihull_-_geograph.org.uk_-_3935079.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kiosk,_Brueton_Park,_Solihull_-_geograph.org.uk_-_3935079.jpg)

Kiosk, Malvern Park, Solihull

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Solihull&action=edit&section=48 "Edit section: Wider borough")]

Other parks across the borough include Elmdon Park, Cole Bank Park, Knowle Park, Langley Hall Park and [Shirley Park](/wiki/Shirley,_West_Midlands "Shirley, West Midlands").

The list of other parks and reserves across the borough includes:

* [Bills Wood](/wiki/Bills_Wood "Bills Wood"), in Bill's Lane, Shirley; 7 hectares (17 acres) of semi natural ancient woodland, designated in 1991.[[117]](#cite_note-SCLNR-117)

* [Smith's Wood](/wiki/Smith%27s_Wood "Smith's Wood") in Windward Way, Smiths Wood; 4.5 hectares (11 acres) of semi natural ancient woodland, designated in 2004.[[117]](#cite_note-SCLNR-117)
* [Yorks Wood](/wiki/Yorks_Wood "Yorks Wood"), in Fordbridge Road, Kingshurst; 10 hectares (25 acres) of semi natural ancient woodland, designated in 1991.[[117]](#cite_note-SCLNR-117)

## Leisure and tourism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Solihull&action=edit&section=49 "Edit section: Leisure and tourism")]

Solihull's Leisure sector has seen growth in recent years, with an average tourism spend in the region per person of £4,081 in 2018.[[118]](#cite_note-118)

Leisure and Sports facilities

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0d/Tudor_Grange_Leisure_Centre%2C_Blossomfield_Road_-_geograph.org.uk_-_2116723.jpg/250px-Tudor_Grange_Leisure_Centre%2C_Blossomfield_Road_-_geograph.org.uk_-_2116723.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tudor_Grange_Leisure_Centre,_Blossomfield_Road_-_geograph.org.uk_-_2116723.jpg)

Tudor Grange Leisure Centre

Solihull has numerous leisure facilities. On the edge of Tudor Grange Park there is a sport centre and public [swimming pool](/wiki/Swimming_pool "Swimming pool") called Tudor Grange Leisure Centre. This pool replaced the old Tudor Grange Sports Centre, which was demolished in 2007, to make way for the brand new leisure centre (A combination of the old Norman Green Athletics Centre and Tudor Grange Sports Centre). This in turn had replaced the outdoor swimming pool – Malvern Park Lido – that had served Solihull from 1954 till its closure in 1982.[[119]](#cite_note-119)

Adjacent to the leisure centre is the Norman Green Athletics Track. The track here was originally opened in 1959 was built of clay and required the rerouting of the Alder Brook stream in Tudor Grange Park, but was upgraded to a full synthetic track in 1985. The upgraded track was named for Councillor Norman Green, Solihull's Deputy Mayor in the late 1970s and in charge of the Appeal fund for the synthetic track. The track is used by the Solihull and Small Heath Athletics Club.[[120]](#cite_note-120)[[121]](#cite_note-121)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d3/Tudor_Grange_Stadium.jpg/250px-Tudor_Grange_Stadium.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tudor_Grange_Stadium.jpg)

Norman Green Athletics Track

At the centre of the Norman Green Athletics Track is the Norman Green Sports Stadium, an association football pitch used by local amateur association football clubs.[[122]](#cite_note-122)

Tudor Grange park also has an 18 hole pitch and putt.[[123]](#cite_note-123)

There is also an outdoor wooden [skateboarding](/wiki/Skateboarding "Skateboarding") and in-line skating facility in Tudor Grange Park.

Entertainment and Activity facilities

On the outskirts of Solihull is the [Bear Grylls Adventure Park](/w/index.php?title=Bear_Grylls_Adventure_Park&action=edit&redlink=1 "Bear Grylls Adventure Park (page does not exist)"), run by [Merlin Entertainments](/wiki/Merlin_Entertainments "Merlin Entertainments") and named after British adventurer [Bear Grylls](/wiki/Bear_Grylls "Bear Grylls"). The centre offers rock climbing, indoor skydiving, a zip line from a Chinook helicopter, Europe's tallest high ropes, an assault course and a chance to dive with sharks.[[124]](#cite_note-124) The adventure park closed in December 2024.[[125]](#cite_note-125)

At the Solihull Land Rover plant is the [Land Rover Experience](/wiki/Solihull_plant#Land_Rover_test_track "Solihull plant") which features a "14 miles of varied terrain on the legendary Jungle Track, Land Track and Adventure Zone" and driving experiences can be booked.[[126]](#cite_note-126)

In woodland on Cut-Throat-Lane in [Hockley Heath](/wiki/Hockley_Heath "Hockley Heath") is the largest paintball centre in the Midlands.[[127]](#cite_note-127)

Clubs and societies

The borough is well served by numerous youth groups, both from the statutory and voluntary sector. There are several Scout groups including [Knowle Sea Scout Group](http://www.knowleseascouts.org.uk) which is based in the south of Solihull and is sponsored by the [Royal Navy](/wiki/Royal_Navy "Royal Navy") providing a wide programme of activities for young people from all over Solihull aged from 6 to 18. The recently refurbished [ice rink](/wiki/Ice_rink "Ice rink") on Hobs Moat Road is home to Solihull's [ice hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey "Ice hockey") teams, the [Solihull Barons](/wiki/Solihull_Barons "Solihull Barons"), Solihull Vikings, a junior ice hockey team, the Mohawks ice racing club, as well as ice dance and figure skating clubs. Above the ice rink was a Riley's snooker club before the company entered administration in 2020 as a result of the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic").

A number of service organisations and other clubs operate in Solihull including a [Round Table](/wiki/Round_Table_(club) "Round Table (club)"), and a [Women's Institute](/wiki/Women%27s_Institutes "Women's Institutes") founded over 90 years ago. There is a [Freemasonic](/wiki/Freemasonry "Freemasonry") temple in Knowle hosting a number of [lodges](/wiki/Masonic_lodge "Masonic lodge").

The [Lunar Society of Birmingham](/wiki/Lunar_Society_of_Birmingham#Modern_Lunar_Societies "Lunar Society of Birmingham"), a local [learned society](/wiki/Learned_society "Learned society") re-founded by [Dame Rachel Waterhouse](/wiki/Rachel_Waterhouse "Rachel Waterhouse"), is headquartered in Shirley, Solihull.[[128]](#cite_note-128)

### Events

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Solihull&action=edit&section=50 "Edit section: Events")]

Every year since the early 1930s (apart from gaps during world wars, and in 2020 due to [COVID-19](/wiki/COVID-19 "COVID-19")[[129]](#cite_note-129)), Solihull Carnival has taken place. This is now fixed to the first weekend after the June half-term and takes place in [Tudor Grange Park](/wiki/Tudor_Grange_Park "Tudor Grange Park"), organised by Shirley Round Table.

Tudor Grange Park is also the venue for the annual free firework display held on the Saturday closest to 5 November, organised by Solihull Round Table. The event attracts about 15,000 people to the park.[[130]](#cite_note-130)

In recent years the town has held [Solihull Summer Festival](/w/index.php?title=Solihull_Summer_Festival&action=edit&redlink=1 "Solihull Summer Festival (page does not exist)") which also takes place in Tudor Grange Park, the festival completed its 7th year in 2023 and is now looking to be a regular addition to the town's summer calendar.

Every year the town plays host to the [Crufts](/wiki/Crufts "Crufts") international dog show and [Horse of the Year Show](/wiki/Horse_of_the_Year_Show "Horse of the Year Show") (HOYS) at its National Exhibition Centre.

The council hosts a popular farmers market every first Friday of the month on the High Street to cater to the increased demand for organic and locally grown food.[[131]](#cite_note-131)

The town hosts an annual [CAMRA](/wiki/CAMRA "CAMRA") beer festival.[[132]](#cite_note-132)

Every year a large unofficial St George's Day Parade takes place, meeting in Solihull, traveling down through neighbouring [Henley-in-Arden](/wiki/Henley-in-Arden "Henley-in-Arden") and into [Stratford-upon-Avon](/wiki/Stratford-upon-Avon "Stratford-upon-Avon").[[133]](#cite_note-133)

## Sport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Solihull&action=edit&section=51 "Edit section: Sport")]

Association football

The largest football club in the town is [Solihull Moors](/wiki/Solihull_Moors_F.C. "Solihull Moors F.C."), who play at Damson Park, 2 miles (3.2 km) from the town centre. The club was established in 2007 following the merger of Solihull Borough and Moor Green and currently play in the [National League](/wiki/National_League_(division) "National League (division)") after being promoted from the [National League North](/wiki/National_League_North "National League North") at the end of the 2015–16 season.

After the demolition of the [Old Wembley Stadium](/wiki/Wembley_Stadium_(1923) "Wembley Stadium (1923)") a bid was made for a new multi-purpose [English National Stadium](/wiki/English_National_Stadium_(West_Midlands) "English National Stadium (West Midlands)") to be built in the north of the borough of Solihull as a new home for English football. The bid was ultimately unsuccessful.

Rugby football

[Birmingham & Solihull R.F.C.](/wiki/Birmingham_%26_Solihull_R.F.C. "Birmingham & Solihull R.F.C."), known as "the Bees", compete in [Midlands 4 West (South)](/wiki/Midlands_4_West_(South) "Midlands 4 West (South)") league as of 2020–21. The club had a brief period in the [RFU Championship](/wiki/RFU_Championship "RFU Championship") (the second tier of English Rugby) in 2010. The club played at a ground in the Solihull town centre at [Sharmans Cross Road](/wiki/Sharmans_Cross_Road "Sharmans Cross Road") until August 2010 when they were relegated from the Championship. Following a brief spell at Solihull Moors' Damson Park ground the club now play at Portway. Other amateur clubs play in the borough at various levels.

Former England Captain and World Cup winner [Martin Johnson](/wiki/Martin_Johnson_(rugby_union) "Martin Johnson (rugby union)") is from Solihull.

Former [Premiership](/wiki/Premiership_Rugby "Premiership Rugby") side [Wasps](/wiki/Wasps_RFC "Wasps RFC") who entered administration and were relegated in 2022 will share Damson Park with Solihull Moors for their return to competitive rugby in the [RFU Championship](/wiki/RFU_Championship "RFU Championship") in 2023.[[134]](#cite_note-134)

Cricket

There are several local level cricket clubs across Solihull, including Hampton and Solihull Cricket Club[[135]](#cite_note-135) and Solihull Municipal Cricket Club,[[136]](#cite_note-136) both of whom compete in the Warwickshire Cricket League and Arden Sunday Cricket League. The [Scorers](/wiki/Scorers,_Shirley "Scorers, Shirley"), on Streetsbrook Road is the home of [Moseley Cricket Club](/wiki/Moseley_Cricket_Club "Moseley Cricket Club"), who played their first recorded match on the ground in 1956.[[137]](#cite_note-137)[[138]](#cite_note-CA-138) As ECB National Club Cricket Championship winners of 1980,[[139]](#cite_note-139) Moseley have achieved numerous championship titles over the years in the [Birmingham and District Premier League](/wiki/Birmingham_and_District_Premier_League "Birmingham and District Premier League").

Equestrian

[Solihull Riding Club](/w/index.php?title=Solihull_Riding_Club&action=edit&redlink=1 "Solihull Riding Club (page does not exist)") is the longest established riding club in the UK. It has one of the largest indoor riding arenas in the country and a premier competition centre.

Solihull is also home to multiple riding schools within its borough, such as [Cottage Farm Stables](/w/index.php?title=Cottage_Farm_Stables&action=edit&redlink=1 "Cottage Farm Stables (page does not exist)") in [Illshaw Heath](/wiki/Illshaw_Heath "Illshaw Heath").

Sailing

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d4/Olton_Mere_%286%29_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1369633.jpg/250px-Olton_Mere_%286%29_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1369633.jpg)](/wiki/File:Olton_Mere_(6)_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1369633.jpg)

Olton Mere Sailing Club

Solihull borough is home to two sailing clubs. The Earlswood Lakes Sailing Club founded in 1960 meet at Windmill Lake amongst the [Earlswood Lakes](/wiki/Earlswood_Lakes "Earlswood Lakes"). The Olton Mere Sailing Club founded in 1926 meet at [Olton Reservoir](/wiki/Olton_Reservoir "Olton Reservoir").

Racquet sports

Tennis is a popular sport in Solihull with courts available to the public in Malvern Park. The Solihull Arden Racquets Club is a thriving racquets and fitness club in Solihull.[[140]](#cite_note-140)

Former UK tennis number one [Jeremy Bates](/wiki/Jeremy_Bates_(tennis) "Jeremy Bates (tennis)") grew up in Solihull and attended Tudor Grange school.

Golf

Solihull has a number of golf clubs and facilities, including Shirley Golf Club, Olton Golf Club, Copt Heath Golf Club, Robin Hood Golf Club, West Midlands Golf Club, Widney Manor Golf Club, Tidbury Green Golf Club, the Arden Course at the Forest of Arden Marriott Hotel & Country Club, an 18-hole pitch and putt at Tudor Grange Park, and a driving range at Four Ashes. Arden Golf Club, Solihull, (now defunct) was founded in 1891. The course was still appearing on maps into the 1930s.[[141]](#cite_note-141)

Archery

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/01/Gates_and_former_workshop%2C_Forest_Hall%2C_Meriden_%28geograph_2590148%29.jpg/250px-Gates_and_former_workshop%2C_Forest_Hall%2C_Meriden_%28geograph_2590148%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gates_and_former_workshop,_Forest_Hall,_Meriden_(geograph_2590148).jpg)

The Gates to Forest Hall, home of the [Woodmen of Arden](/wiki/Woodmen_of_Arden "Woodmen of Arden")

Archery has always been a prominent sport in Solihull. The Meriden Archery Club, founded in 1936, meet within the grounds of the Packington Estate and practice [target archery](/wiki/Target_archery "Target archery"). The Forest of Arden Bowmen practice [field archery](/wiki/Field_archery "Field archery") in grounds nearby.

Meriden is also home to the [Woodmen of the Forest of Arden](/wiki/Woodmen_of_Arden "Woodmen of Arden") founded 1785, an illustrious society of archers who shoot a unique and historic type of [clout archery](/wiki/Clout_archery "Clout archery") with longbows.

Cycling

Solihull is also home to Solihull Cycling Club which was founded in 1929. The club has produced National Champions, Olympic Medallists and Tour de France riders[[142]](#cite_note-142)

Meriden is home to [the National Cyclists Memorial](/wiki/Meriden,_West_Midlands#National_Cyclists_Memorial "Meriden, West Midlands"), dedicated to the cyclists who died in the First World War. National cycling organisations commemorate these deaths with an annual mid-May service on the green.

Swimming

Solihull Swimming Club is based at Tudor Grange Leisure Centre. First established in 1963,[[143]](#cite_note-143) the club now boasts over 600 members and also runs [water polo](/wiki/Water_polo "Water polo") teams.

Hockey

[Solihull Barons](/wiki/Solihull_Barons "Solihull Barons") are the local ice hockey team and play their home games at the Solihull Ice Rink.

Solihull also has a number of field hockey clubs, namely [Old Silhillians Hockey Club](/w/index.php?title=Old_Silhillians_Hockey_Club&action=edit&redlink=1 "Old Silhillians Hockey Club (page does not exist)"), Olton & West Warwickshire Hockey Club and Solihull Blossomfield Hockey Club.

Other

[Gaelic games](/wiki/Gaelic_games "Gaelic games") are played by Warwickshire GAA who play their home matches in [Páirc na hÉireann](/wiki/P%C3%A1irc_na_h%C3%89ireann "Páirc na hÉireann") in Solihull.

The town has an indoor bowling area and club.

## In popular culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Solihull&action=edit&section=55 "Edit section: In popular culture")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This section **may contain [irrelevant](/wiki/Wikipedia:What_Wikipedia_is_not#Wikipedia_is_not_an_indiscriminate_collection_of_information "Wikipedia:What Wikipedia is not") references to [popular culture](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Trivia_sections#"In_popular_culture"_and_"Cultural_references"_material "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Trivia sections")**. Please help [improve it](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Solihull&action=edit) by removing such content and adding [citations](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources") to [reliable](/wiki/Wikipedia:Reliable_sources "Wikipedia:Reliable sources"), [independent sources](/wiki/Wikipedia:Independent_sources "Wikipedia:Independent sources"). *(March 2024)* |

[William Camden](/wiki/William_Camden "William Camden") visited Solihull in 1558 and said of it in his work *Britannia*: "I saw Solyhill; but in it, setting aside the church, there is nothing worth sight."[[148]](#cite_note-148)

In [David Turner](/wiki/David_Turner_(dramatist) "David Turner (dramatist)")'s 1962 play *[Semi-Detached](/wiki/Semi-Detached_(play) "Semi-Detached (play)")*, Solihull is parodied as Dowlihull, a fictional town where the protagonist aspires to live.[[149]](#cite_note-149)

In the British political TV series *[Yes Minister](/wiki/Yes_Minister "Yes Minister")*, in the S1E07 1980 episode "Jobs For The Boys" a public-private partnership project is called the "Solihull Project".[[150]](#cite_note-150)[*[importance?](/wiki/Wikipedia:What_Wikipedia_is_not#Encyclopedic_content "Wikipedia:What Wikipedia is not")*]

In Episode 2, Season 2 of the British comedy [*The Thin Blue Line*](/wiki/The_Thin_Blue_Line_(British_TV_series) "The Thin Blue Line (British TV series)"), Detective Inspector Derek Grim ([David Haig](/wiki/David_Haig "David Haig")) suggests a [Martian](/wiki/Martian "Martian") seeking asylum in the UK marry "a sweet girl from Solihull".[[151]](#cite_note-151)[*[importance?](/wiki/Wikipedia:What_Wikipedia_is_not#Encyclopedic_content "Wikipedia:What Wikipedia is not")*]

In Episode 6, Season 15 of the British motoring show [*Top Gear*](/wiki/Top_Gear_(2002_TV_series) "Top Gear (2002 TV series)"), presenters [Jeremy Clarkson](/wiki/Jeremy_Clarkson "Jeremy Clarkson"), [James May](/wiki/James_May "James May") and [Richard Hammond](/wiki/Richard_Hammond "Richard Hammond") travel to Solihull, the birthplace of Richard Hammond, where they test the "waterproof-ness" of their classic British sports cars in a carwash.[[152]](#cite_note-152)

Episode 3, Series 2 of comedy series *[The Kevin Bishop Show](/wiki/The_Kevin_Bishop_Show "The Kevin Bishop Show")* features a sketch about a British remake of the award winning US television series [*Lost*](/wiki/Lost_(2004_TV_series) "Lost (2004 TV series)"), in which a [coach](/wiki/Coach_(bus) "Coach (bus)") of well spoken people crash onto a [traffic island](/wiki/Roundabout "Roundabout") in Solihull town centre.[[153]](#cite_note-153)

The steam engine used as the [Hogwarts Express](/wiki/Hogwarts#Hogwarts_Express "Hogwarts") in the *[Harry Potter](/wiki/Harry_Potter_(film_series) "Harry Potter (film series)")* film franchise was the [GWR 4900 Class 5972 Olton Hall](/wiki/GWR_4900_Class_5972_Olton_Hall "GWR 4900 Class 5972 Olton Hall"), named for the now demolished Olton Hall [estate](/wiki/English_country_house "English country house") in Olton, Solihull.[[154]](#cite_note-154)

In the S6E05 episode of television series [*Peaky Blinders*](/wiki/Peaky_Blinders_(TV_series) "Peaky Blinders (TV series)") the main character [Tommy Shelby](/wiki/Tommy_Shelby "Tommy Shelby") meets [aristocrat](/wiki/Aristocracy_(class) "Aristocracy (class)") [Diana Mitford](/wiki/Diana_Mosley "Diana Mosley"), who is staying at a hotel in Solihull, to secure funding from The Guinness Trust – managed by her ex-husband.[[155]](#cite_note-155)
